title: Accountants and auditors
rationale: Accounting work is predominantly digital and cognitive, involving data analysis, financial reporting, and compliance checking - all areas where AI tools are already making significant impact by automating routine tasks like data entry, basic calculations, and standard report generation. However, the profession requires regulatory compliance, client interaction, strategic advisory work, and professional judgment that AI cannot fully replace. Demand elasticity is moderate because while cheaper accounting services could expand access for small businesses and enable more frequent financial analysis, growth is constrained by regulatory requirements, business formation rates, and the fact that most entities already have mandatory accounting needs. The occupation will survive but require substantial skill shifts toward advisory, analytical, and strategic roles as routine tasks become automated.

title: Actors
rationale: AI significantly disrupts acting through deepfakes, digital doubles, voice synthesis, and virtual performers that can handle certain roles, especially background work, commercials, and some lead roles in digital media. However, the core human elements of live performance, authentic emotional expression, and audience connection remain largely irreplaceable. Demand elasticity is moderate - while cheaper digital actors could expand content creation in some areas (like personalized content, indie films, or interactive media), traditional entertainment demand is somewhat constrained by audience attention and cultural factors, though new formats may emerge.

title: Actuaries
rationale: Actuaries work primarily with data analysis, statistical modeling, and risk assessment - all core AI capabilities that are rapidly advancing. AI can already perform sophisticated statistical analysis, pattern recognition in large datasets, and predictive modeling that forms the foundation of actuarial work. However, demand for actuarial services is relatively inelastic because insurance needs are largely fixed by regulatory requirements, population demographics, and existing business structures - companies don't suddenly need 10x more risk assessments just because they become cheaper to produce. The combination of high AI disruption potential with constrained demand expansion suggests significant headcount reduction as AI tools enable fewer actuaries to handle the same workload.

title: Administrative services and facilities managers
rationale: Administrative services and facilities managers have moderate disruption potential as AI can assist with data analysis, scheduling, compliance monitoring, and some administrative tasks, but the core work requires physical oversight of facilities, staff management, and real-world problem-solving that demands human presence and judgment. Demand elasticity is low because organizations have relatively fixed needs for facility management and administrative oversight - making these services 10x cheaper wouldn't cause companies to dramatically expand their office space or administrative infrastructure. Since disruption is at the boundary (6) but doesn't reach the high threshold of 7+, this occupation falls into the resilient category where AI enhances some tasks but doesn't fundamentally reshape the role.

title: Adult basic and secondary education and ESL teachers
rationale: AI significantly disrupts the knowledge-work portions of teaching through personalized learning platforms, automated assessment, and adaptive curriculum delivery, but core human elements like cultural sensitivity, motivation, and one-on-one support remain essential. Demand elasticity is high because adult education is currently constrained by cost and accessibility - AI-powered tools could dramatically expand access to basic education, ESL instruction, and GED preparation for underserved populations globally. While AI will transform how these teachers work, the massive latent demand for affordable adult education means employment could grow despite productivity gains.

title: Advertising sales agents
rationale: Advertising sales involves predominantly cognitive tasks like client research, proposal creation, presentation development, and relationship management that AI can increasingly automate or significantly enhance. AI tools already assist with lead generation, personalized outreach, proposal writing, and market analysis. The elasticity is low because advertising budgets are typically fixed or constrained by business revenue - companies don't dramatically increase advertising spend just because sales processes become more efficient. The existing 6% projected decline in employment reflects structural shifts toward automated digital ad placement, confirming that productivity gains won't translate to expanded demand for human sales agents.

title: Advertising, promotions, and marketing managers
rationale: Marketing and advertising managers face high disruption as AI transforms core tasks like market research, campaign optimization, content creation, and data analysis - areas that are predominantly digital and cognitive. AI tools already materially assist with audience targeting, A/B testing, creative generation, and performance analytics. However, demand elasticity is high because cheaper, faster, and more precise marketing capabilities unlock significant latent demand - small businesses can access sophisticated marketing previously available only to large corporations, and the explosion of digital channels creates more opportunities for targeted campaigns. The net effect is augmentation as productivity gains enable each manager to handle more campaigns and clients rather than reducing headcount.

title: Aerospace engineering and operations technologists and technicians
rationale: While AI can assist with data analysis, computer simulations, and some design optimization tasks, the core work involves hands-on installation, calibration, and maintenance of physical aerospace equipment and test facilities. The occupation requires significant physical presence for assembly work, equipment operation, and safety monitoring that AI cannot replace. Demand elasticity is low because aerospace testing and manufacturing requirements are driven by regulatory compliance and safety standards rather than cost considerations - even if AI made certain tasks faster, the number of aircraft designs and required testing procedures wouldn't dramatically increase. The disruption score of 6 places this in the resilient category where AI augments but doesn't fundamentally reshape the core work.

title: Aerospace engineers
rationale: Aerospace engineering involves substantial digital design work, simulation, analysis, and optimization that AI can significantly enhance - from aerodynamic modeling to structural analysis to systems integration. However, the work requires deep domain expertise, safety-critical decision making, and integration with physical testing and manufacturing processes that maintain human oversight requirements. Demand elasticity is moderate because while AI-accelerated design could enable more aerospace projects and faster innovation cycles (especially in emerging areas like small satellites and drones), the industry is heavily regulated, capital-intensive, and constrained by physical testing requirements and certification processes. The combination of high disruption to core technical tasks but moderate demand expansion due to regulatory and capital constraints places this in the restructure category, where engineers who adapt to AI-augmented workflows will thrive while those who don't may be displaced.

title: Agricultural and food science technicians
rationale: AI significantly disrupts the analytical and data processing aspects of this work - sample analysis, test result compilation, report generation, and pattern recognition in crop/food data are all areas where AI tools are already proving valuable. However, the physical components (sample collection, equipment operation, laboratory maintenance) and the need for hands-on expertise in agricultural/food systems remain important. Demand elasticity is high because cheaper, faster agricultural and food testing could unlock significant latent demand - more frequent soil testing, expanded food safety monitoring, broader crop disease surveillance, and enhanced quality control across smaller operations that currently can't afford comprehensive testing services.

title: Agricultural and food scientists
rationale: Agricultural and food scientists face high disruption as AI can significantly enhance their core knowledge work including data analysis, research design, genetic analysis, and predictive modeling for crop yields and food safety. However, the work still requires substantial fieldwork, laboratory experimentation, and physical sample collection that AI cannot replace. Demand elasticity is high because cheaper and faster agricultural research could unlock massive latent demand - more crops could be optimized, more food safety testing conducted, and personalized nutrition solutions developed at scale, especially as global food security challenges intensify. The combination of high disruption with high elasticity suggests workers will become dramatically more productive while total demand expands.

title: Agricultural engineers
rationale: Agricultural engineers face high disruption as their core work involves computer-aided design, data analysis, systems optimization, and integration of AI/geospatial technologies - all areas where AI tools are rapidly advancing and already providing material assistance. The elasticity is high because cheaper, faster agricultural engineering solutions can unlock massive latent demand globally - more efficient farming systems, precision agriculture, and automated technologies become accessible to smaller operations and developing regions when costs drop dramatically. Despite the high disruption, the combination of growing global food security needs and AI-enabled cost reductions suggests demand expansion will absorb productivity gains, leading to augmentation rather than replacement.

title: Agricultural workers
rationale: Agricultural work remains predominantly physical and hands-on, requiring real-world presence for tasks like planting, harvesting, animal care, and equipment operation in unpredictable outdoor environments. While AI may assist with some record-keeping and equipment automation, the core work of crop cultivation and livestock management requires human dexterity, physical strength, and adaptability to weather and biological systems. Demand for agricultural products is relatively inelastic - people don't consume dramatically more food if production becomes cheaper, and regulatory/biological constraints limit how much productivity gains can expand total agricultural employment. The occupation shows resilience as AI touches only peripheral aspects while core physical tasks remain human-dependent.

title: Air traffic controllers
rationale: Air traffic control involves significant cognitive work (monitoring radar, making quick decisions, coordinating multiple aircraft) that AI could potentially assist with, but the core responsibility requires real-time human judgment for safety-critical decisions in unpredictable situations with human lives at stake. The work is heavily regulated and requires immediate physical presence in control towers and facilities. Demand for air traffic control is essentially fixed by the number of flights and airports - even if AI made controllers 10x more efficient, we wouldn't suddenly have 10x more air traffic, and safety regulations would likely maintain minimum staffing requirements regardless of technological capabilities.

title: Aircraft and avionics equipment mechanics and technicians
rationale: This occupation scores low on disruption because the core work is predominantly hands-on physical maintenance, repair, and inspection of aircraft components that requires direct manipulation of mechanical systems, working in uncomfortable positions, and real-world problem-solving with safety-critical equipment. While AI might assist with some diagnostic data interpretation or maintenance scheduling, the fundamental tasks of physically repairing engines, replacing parts, and conducting safety inspections cannot be automated. Elasticity is low because aircraft maintenance demand is largely fixed by regulatory requirements, fleet size, and flight hours - even if AI made diagnostics faster, the same number of aircraft still need the same physical maintenance cycles. The net effect is resilient because AI touches only peripheral aspects like documentation or diagnostic assistance while leaving the core mechanical work unchanged.

title: Airline and commercial pilots
rationale: While AI assists with flight planning, weather analysis, and navigation systems, the core piloting tasks remain fundamentally physical and require real-time human judgment for safety-critical decisions, emergency response, and aircraft control. Current autopilot technology handles routine flight phases but pilots must actively monitor systems and take control during takeoffs, landings, and emergencies. Demand elasticity is low because air travel is largely driven by economic factors and route capacity rather than pilot costs, and regulatory requirements mandate human pilots for passenger safety. The occupation remains resilient as AI enhances existing systems rather than replacing the essential human oversight role.

title: Animal care and service workers
rationale: Animal care work is fundamentally physical and hands-on, requiring direct interaction with living animals for feeding, grooming, exercising, and monitoring health - tasks that AI cannot perform. While AI might assist with peripheral administrative tasks like scheduling or record-keeping, the core work of animal handling, physical care, and real-time behavioral assessment remains entirely human-dependent. Demand shows moderate elasticity as lower costs could expand access to services like pet grooming, training, and boarding, but the physical nature of the work means productivity gains from AI would be minimal. The low disruption score keeps this occupation in the resilient category despite moderate demand elasticity.

title: Announcers and DJs
rationale: AI disruption is high because core tasks like voice synthesis, music selection, content research, and script writing are directly in AI's path - AI DJs and automated programming already exist and are improving rapidly. The work is predominantly digital/cognitive rather than requiring physical presence. Demand elasticity is low because radio listening time and event hosting needs are relatively fixed - people don't consume dramatically more DJ services just because they're cheaper, and many stations are already moving toward automated programming to cut costs rather than expand content.

title: Anthropologists and archeologists
rationale: AI moderately disrupts anthropology/archaeology through tools for data analysis, pattern recognition in artifacts, and research assistance, but core work remains fundamentally human-centered - fieldwork, cultural interpretation, and contextual understanding of human behavior require physical presence and nuanced judgment. Demand elasticity is low because the work is largely driven by academic research funding, regulatory compliance requirements, and corporate consulting needs that don't scale dramatically with cost reductions. While AI can enhance analytical capabilities, the occupation's reliance on fieldwork, cultural sensitivity, and human interpretation keeps disruption at moderate levels, making it resilient overall.

title: Arbitrators, mediators, and conciliators
rationale: The disruption score is 6 because while AI can assist with document analysis, case research, and drafting settlement agreements, the core work requires human judgment, emotional intelligence, and interpersonal skills to facilitate dialogue between conflicting parties. The elasticity score is 3 because demand for dispute resolution is largely constrained by the number of actual disputes that arise, not by cost or speed - people don't create more conflicts just because resolution becomes cheaper. Since disruption is at the boundary (6) but the work fundamentally requires human presence and emotional intelligence for managing heated disputes, this occupation remains resilient to AI displacement.

title: Architects
rationale: Architecture involves substantial digital work including CAD/BIM modeling, design documentation, and project coordination that AI can increasingly assist with or automate, earning a disruption score of 7. However, the profession still requires creative vision, client interaction, site visits, and regulatory compliance that remain human-centered. Demand elasticity is moderate (6) because while cheaper architectural services could unlock some latent demand for design work and enable more frequent renovations or smaller projects, the construction industry's structural constraints, regulatory requirements, and the discrete nature of building projects limit how much total demand can expand. This combination of high disruption with moderate elasticity places architecture in the restructure category, where the profession will survive but require significant adaptation to work alongside AI tools.

title: Architectural and engineering managers
rationale: Architectural and engineering managers face high disruption as AI transforms core knowledge work including project planning, budget analysis, resource allocation, and technical oversight - areas where AI tools are already proving valuable. However, the role retains essential human elements in leadership, team coordination, stakeholder management, and complex decision-making that require interpersonal skills and contextual judgment. Demand elasticity is moderate because while AI-enhanced efficiency could enable more projects and better resource utilization, growth is constrained by physical infrastructure timelines, regulatory processes, and organizational hierarchies that limit how quickly management demand can expand.

title: Archivists, curators, and museum workers
rationale: AI moderately disrupts this occupation by automating digital cataloging, metadata creation, and basic research tasks, but the core work requires physical handling of artifacts, expert authentication, curatorial judgment, and public engagement that remains human-centered. Demand elasticity is low because institutional budgets for cultural preservation are relatively fixed, and while AI might make some tasks cheaper, it won't dramatically expand the number of museums or archives society supports. The disruption score of 6 places this just at the boundary, but the fundamentally human-centered nature of authentication, preservation expertise, and cultural interpretation keeps this in resilient territory.

title: Art directors
rationale: Art directors face high disruption as AI tools now generate images, layouts, and design concepts that previously required human creativity and technical skill. Core tasks like visual concept development, design approval, and creative direction are increasingly AI-assisted. However, demand elasticity is high because cheaper, faster creative production unlocks significant latent demand - more businesses can afford professional-quality visual campaigns, content creators need more diverse assets, and digital platforms require constant visual content. The role evolves toward AI-augmented creative leadership rather than replacement, as human judgment in brand strategy, client relations, and creative vision remains essential.

title: Assemblers and fabricators
rationale: Assembly and fabrication work remains predominantly physical and hands-on, requiring manual dexterity, physical presence, and real-world problem-solving that AI cannot replicate. While some peripheral tasks like reading blueprints or quality control documentation might benefit from AI assistance, the core work of physically manipulating, positioning, and assembling components is beyond current AI capabilities. Demand for assembled products is relatively inelastic - consumers and businesses don't dramatically increase purchases of cars, appliances, or aircraft just because assembly becomes cheaper, as these are durable goods with replacement cycles driven by need rather than cost. The BLS projection of -1% employment decline reflects automation trends rather than AI disruption, confirming this occupation's resilience to AI-driven change.

title: Athletes and sports competitors
rationale: Athletes and sports competitors have minimal AI disruption because their core work is entirely physical performance, competition, and skill demonstration that requires human presence and cannot be digitized or automated. While AI might assist with peripheral tasks like performance analysis or training optimization, the fundamental job of competing athletically remains unchanged. Demand elasticity is low because sports entertainment consumption is relatively fixed - fans don't watch 10x more games just because athlete performance improves, and league structures limit expansion opportunities. The physical, performance-based nature of athletic competition makes this occupation highly resilient to AI disruption.

title: Athletic trainers
rationale: Athletic trainers' core work is predominantly hands-on physical assessment, injury treatment, and real-time emergency response that requires human presence and tactile skills, limiting AI's direct impact on primary duties. While AI could assist with administrative tasks like record-keeping and injury analysis, the essential functions of applying treatments, providing emergency care, and developing rehabilitation programs remain fundamentally human-centered. Demand shows moderate elasticity as cheaper/faster services could expand access to athletic training in underserved markets and youth sports programs. However, with disruption at only moderate levels, this occupation remains resilient to AI transformation.

title: Atmospheric scientists, including meteorologists
rationale: Atmospheric science involves significant computational modeling and data analysis that AI can enhance, but core work requires domain expertise in interpreting complex atmospheric phenomena, field data collection, and real-time decision-making during severe weather events. While AI tools can improve forecasting models and automate some data processing, the profession demands deep scientific understanding and human judgment for critical weather warnings. Demand elasticity is low because weather forecasting needs are relatively fixed - businesses and the public don't consume dramatically more weather services just because they're cheaper or faster, and much employment depends on government funding with structural constraints.

title: Audiologists
rationale: Audiologists face moderate disruption as AI can assist with hearing test analysis, pattern recognition in audiograms, and treatment planning, but the core work requires hands-on patient examination, device fitting, counseling, and physical procedures that demand human presence and interpersonal skills. Demand elasticity is high because cheaper and more accessible hearing diagnostics could expand services to underserved populations, enable more frequent monitoring, and support preventive care programs. However, since disruption remains moderate (5), this occupation falls into the resilient category where AI enhances rather than fundamentally reshapes the core work.

title: Automotive body and glass repairers
rationale: This occupation involves predominantly hands-on physical work including hammering dents, welding parts, handling glass, and working in cramped positions around vehicles. While AI might assist with damage assessment and cost estimation, the core tasks of body repair, glass installation, and structural realignment require manual dexterity, physical strength, and real-world problem-solving that AI cannot replicate. Demand elasticity is low because vehicle accidents and glass damage occur at relatively fixed rates regardless of repair costs, and people typically need only necessary repairs rather than elective services. The physical, location-specific nature of the work combined with inelastic demand makes this occupation resilient to AI disruption.

title: Automotive service technicians and mechanics
rationale: Automotive service work remains fundamentally hands-on and physical, requiring direct manipulation of mechanical components, lifting heavy parts, and working in uncomfortable positions under vehicles. While AI-powered diagnostic tools are increasingly used to identify problems, the core work of actually repairing, replacing, and maintaining vehicle components cannot be automated. Demand for automotive repair services is relatively inelastic - vehicle owners need repairs when problems occur regardless of cost, and people don't suddenly get 10x more repairs just because diagnostic tools become more efficient. The moderate disruption from better diagnostic capabilities combined with inelastic demand keeps this occupation in the resilient category.

title: Bakers
rationale: Baking involves predominantly physical tasks like measuring, mixing, kneading, shaping dough, and monitoring ovens that require hands-on manipulation and real-time sensory judgment that AI cannot replicate. While AI might assist with peripheral tasks like inventory management or recipe optimization, the core craft of baking remains fundamentally manual and experiential. Demand for baked goods is relatively inelastic as it's tied to basic dietary needs and population growth rather than cost sensitivity - people don't dramatically increase bread consumption if production becomes cheaper. The combination of low disruption potential and inelastic demand makes this occupation resilient to AI displacement.

title: Barbers, hairstylists, and cosmetologists
rationale: This occupation scores low on disruption because the core work is fundamentally hands-on and physical - cutting, styling, coloring hair requires manual dexterity, spatial awareness, and real-time adaptation to each client's unique hair texture and face shape. While AI might assist with peripheral tasks like scheduling or trend analysis, the tactile nature of hair manipulation and the need for immediate physical presence makes this work largely immune to AI disruption. Elasticity is moderate-low because while cheaper services might increase frequency of visits somewhat, demand for haircuts and styling is relatively stable and tied to personal grooming needs rather than being heavily price-constrained. The resilient category fits because AI cannot meaningfully change the core manual work of this occupation.

title: Bartenders
rationale: Bartending scores low on disruption because the core work is fundamentally physical and interpersonal - mixing drinks by hand, engaging with customers face-to-face, monitoring intoxication levels, and managing the physical bar environment. While AI might help with inventory management or payment processing, it cannot replicate the manual dexterity, real-time customer interaction, and physical presence required. Elasticity is low because demand for bartending services is relatively fixed - people don't dramatically increase their bar visits or drink consumption just because service becomes more efficient. The occupation remains resilient as AI cannot meaningfully transform the core human-centered, physical aspects of bartending work.

title: Bill and account collectors
rationale: Bill and account collectors face very high disruption as their core tasks—contacting debtors, negotiating payment plans, tracking accounts, and maintaining records—are predominantly digital and language-based activities that AI can increasingly handle through automated calling systems, chatbots, and intelligent debt management software. The BLS already notes that enhanced software and automated systems are increasing productivity and allowing fewer employees to handle more accounts. Demand elasticity is very low because the volume of debt collection is fundamentally constrained by the amount of overdue debt in the economy, not by the cost or speed of collection services—making debt collection 10x cheaper doesn't create 10x more debt to collect. The combination of high AI disruption and inelastic demand points to significant workforce contraction, which aligns with the BLS projection of -10% employment decline through 2034.

title: Biochemists and biophysicists
rationale: AI significantly disrupts biochemistry and biophysics through automated data analysis, protein structure prediction, molecular modeling, and literature review - core knowledge work activities that comprise much of the daily work. However, physical laboratory experimentation, complex hypothesis generation, and interdisciplinary collaboration still require human expertise. Demand elasticity is high because AI-accelerated research dramatically reduces the cost and time for drug discovery, genetic analysis, and biotechnology development, unlocking massive latent demand from pharmaceutical companies, biotech firms, and research institutions seeking faster innovation cycles. The combination of high disruption with high elasticity suggests workers will become dramatically more productive rather than displaced.

title: Bioengineers and biomedical engineers
rationale: Biomedical engineers face high disruption as AI increasingly transforms their core tasks including device design, statistical modeling, simulation development, and research analysis - areas where AI tools are already proving highly capable. However, demand elasticity is high because cheaper and faster biomedical engineering could dramatically expand access to medical devices, personalized treatments, and healthcare innovations globally, particularly in underserved markets where cost has been a major barrier. The net effect is augmentation as the healthcare sector's massive unmet needs and regulatory approval processes will absorb productivity gains through expanded innovation rather than workforce reduction.

title: Biological technicians
rationale: AI significantly disrupts the data analysis, documentation, and experimental design aspects of biological technician work, which comprise a substantial portion of their duties, but the core laboratory work remains hands-on and requires physical manipulation of samples and equipment. Demand elasticity is high because cheaper and faster biological analysis could unlock significant research that is currently constrained by cost and time - more drug discovery, environmental monitoring, and biotechnology applications would become feasible if analysis costs dropped dramatically. The combination of meaningful disruption with high demand expansion suggests workers will become more productive while total employment remains stable or grows.

title: Boilermakers
rationale: Boilermakers perform highly physical, hands-on work in challenging environments that requires manual dexterity, physical presence, and real-world problem-solving in confined spaces and at heights. While they read blueprints and may use some automated welding equipment, the core work involves manual assembly, installation, maintenance, and repair of large industrial equipment that cannot be done remotely or digitally. Demand for boilermaker services is relatively inelastic because it's tied to fixed infrastructure needs - existing boilers require maintenance regardless of cost, and new installations are driven by industrial capacity rather than price sensitivity. The projected 2% employment decline reflects structural shifts away from coal power rather than AI displacement.

title: Bookkeeping, accounting, and auditing clerks
rationale: This occupation scores high on disruption (8) because the core tasks—data entry, transaction recording, reconciliation, and report generation—are predominantly digital and routine, with AI and automation already significantly changing how this work is done through specialized software. The elasticity score is low (3) because demand for bookkeeping services is relatively fixed; businesses need a certain amount of financial record-keeping regardless of cost, and making it cheaper doesn't create substantially more demand for these services. The BLS already projects a 6% decline due to automation, confirming this is a 'replace' scenario where AI absorbs core work without compensating demand expansion.

title: Broadcast, sound, and video technicians
rationale: AI significantly disrupts the digital aspects of this work - automated audio/video editing, intelligent mixing, real-time processing, and equipment monitoring are already emerging, affecting core technical tasks. However, the work still requires substantial physical setup, live event management, and real-world problem-solving that keeps disruption from being higher. Demand elasticity is high because cheaper, faster audio/video production unlocks massive latent demand - more content creation, live streaming, corporate video, and immersive experiences become economically viable as AI reduces production costs and complexity. The combination suggests workers will produce dramatically more output rather than be displaced.

title: Budget analysts
rationale: Budget analysts perform predominantly knowledge work involving data analysis, financial modeling, report writing, and numerical computation - all areas where AI tools are already materially useful and rapidly improving. AI can automate much of the data processing, variance analysis, and report generation that forms the core of budget analysis work. However, demand elasticity is low because organizations have relatively fixed budgeting needs regardless of cost - they don't suddenly need 10x more budget analyses just because AI makes them cheaper or faster. The combination of high AI disruption potential with inelastic demand suggests significant headcount contraction as AI absorbs much of the analytical workload without proportional demand expansion.

title: Bus drivers
rationale: Bus driving scores low on disruption because it remains fundamentally a physical, real-world occupation requiring human presence, safety judgment, and passenger interaction - while autonomous vehicles exist in testing, full deployment for passenger transport faces significant regulatory, safety, and infrastructure barriers that will take decades to resolve. Elasticity is low because demand for bus transportation is relatively fixed by population, school enrollment, and existing transit routes - making buses 10x more efficient wouldn't dramatically increase ridership as people's transportation needs are fairly inelastic. The resilient category fits because AI disruption remains minimal in the near term, with the core work of safely operating vehicles and managing passengers remaining human-centered.

title: Butchers
rationale: Butchering is fundamentally a physical, hands-on craft requiring manual dexterity, knife skills, and tactile judgment to cut, trim, and prepare meat - tasks that remain largely beyond AI's capabilities. While AI might assist with peripheral functions like inventory management or ordering, the core work of meat cutting, customer service in retail settings, and maintaining food safety standards requires human presence and skill. Demand for butcher services is relatively inelastic as meat consumption patterns are stable and consumers don't dramatically increase meat purchases when processing becomes marginally cheaper. The low disruption score combined with inelastic demand places this occupation firmly in the resilient category.

title: Calibration technologists and technicians
rationale: Calibration work involves significant hands-on physical manipulation of precision instruments, equipment inspection, and real-world testing that requires human dexterity and judgment, limiting AI's ability to disrupt core tasks. While AI could assist with data recording, analysis, and scheduling, the fundamental work of physically adjusting, testing, and maintaining measurement devices remains largely manual. Demand for calibration services is relatively inelastic as it's driven by regulatory requirements, safety standards, and existing equipment maintenance needs rather than cost considerations - organizations need their instruments calibrated regardless of price. The moderate disruption score combined with low elasticity places this occupation in the resilient category, where AI touches peripheral tasks but doesn't reshape the core work.

title: Cardiovascular technologists and technicians
rationale: The disruption score is moderate because while AI can assist with analyzing test results and identifying abnormalities in cardiovascular data, the core work requires direct patient interaction, equipment operation, and physical positioning of patients during procedures. The work involves significant hands-on technical skills and patient care that cannot be digitized. The elasticity score is low because demand for cardiovascular testing is primarily driven by medical necessity and population health needs rather than cost - people don't get more heart tests simply because they become cheaper, and the aging population creates steady but not explosive demand growth. With moderate disruption and low elasticity, this occupation falls into the resilient category where AI enhances some analytical aspects but doesn't fundamentally reshape the core work.

title: Career and technical education teachers
rationale: CTE teachers have moderate disruption because while AI can assist with lesson planning, grading, and administrative tasks, the core work requires hands-on demonstration, equipment supervision, safety oversight, and direct mentorship in physical skills like welding, automotive repair, or culinary arts. The elasticity is high because cheaper, AI-enhanced vocational training could unlock significant demand - more workers need technical skills as automation changes job markets, and personalized skill development could expand access to career training. However, since disruption remains moderate at 5, this occupation falls into the resilient category where AI enhances rather than reshapes the fundamental teaching and mentoring work.

title: Carpenters
rationale: Carpentry remains fundamentally a hands-on, physical craft requiring precise manual work, real-world problem-solving, and adaptation to unpredictable job site conditions. While AI assists with peripheral tasks like blueprint reading, measurements, and project planning through apps and tablets, the core work of cutting, shaping, installing, and assembling materials requires human dexterity, spatial reasoning, and physical presence. Demand for carpentry services is relatively inelastic - construction needs are driven by population growth and infrastructure requirements rather than cost sensitivity, and even dramatic productivity improvements wouldn't significantly expand total construction volume. The occupation fits the resilient category as AI touches only the administrative periphery while leaving the essential craft skills unchanged.

title: Cartographers and photogrammetrists
rationale: AI significantly disrupts cartography through automated satellite image analysis, pattern recognition in geographic data, and AI-powered map generation tools that can process vast datasets much faster than humans. However, demand elasticity is high because cheaper, faster mapping enables expanded applications in urban planning, environmental monitoring, disaster response, autonomous vehicles, and location-based services that were previously cost-prohibitive. The combination of high disruption with strong demand expansion suggests workers will produce dramatically more output rather than be replaced.

title: Cashiers
rationale: Cashier work is highly digital and routine - scanning items, processing payments, and managing transactions through point-of-sale systems - making it highly susceptible to AI and automation disruption, as evidenced by the widespread adoption of self-checkout systems. Demand for cashier services is extremely inelastic because the number of transactions is determined by customer purchasing behavior, not by the cost or speed of checkout processing - faster checkout doesn't make people buy more groceries or gas. The BLS already projects a 10% decline in cashier employment specifically due to technological advances, confirming this is a classic replacement scenario where automation absorbs the work without expanding total demand.

title: Chefs and head cooks
rationale: Chefs and head cooks have moderate disruption because while AI can assist with menu planning, recipe development, inventory management, and administrative tasks, the core work remains fundamentally physical and sensory - tasting, cooking, supervising kitchen operations, and ensuring food quality require human presence and expertise. The elasticity is moderate because cheaper food preparation through AI efficiency could expand demand for dining experiences and allow more establishments to offer chef-driven menus, but growth is constrained by physical dining capacity and consumer spending patterns. Since disruption is moderate (4), this occupation falls into the resilient category despite moderate elasticity.

title: Chemical engineers
rationale: Chemical engineers face moderate disruption as AI can assist with design optimization, process modeling, and data analysis, but the core work requires deep understanding of physical processes, safety protocols, and hands-on problem-solving in manufacturing environments. Demand elasticity is low because chemical production is largely driven by existing market needs for specific products rather than cost constraints - making processes 10x cheaper doesn't create 10x demand for gasoline or detergents. The disruption score of 6 places this occupation in the resilient category, where AI enhances capabilities but doesn't fundamentally reshape the profession's core requirements.

title: Chemical technicians
rationale: Chemical technicians face moderate disruption as AI can assist with data analysis, report generation, and some experimental design, but the core work requires hands-on laboratory skills, equipment operation, and physical sample handling that AI cannot replace. Demand elasticity is low because chemical testing and quality control requirements are largely fixed by regulatory standards and manufacturing needs - faster analysis doesn't typically create demand for dramatically more testing. The physical, hands-on nature of laboratory work and equipment maintenance keeps this occupation in the resilient category despite some AI-assisted improvements in data processing tasks.

title: Chemists and materials scientists
rationale: AI significantly disrupts the computational and analytical aspects of chemistry and materials science - molecular modeling, data analysis, literature review, and experimental design are increasingly AI-assisted, while laboratory work remains hands-on. The elasticity is high because cheaper and faster chemical research unlocks substantial latent demand across pharmaceuticals, materials development, environmental solutions, and emerging fields like nanotechnology where cost barriers currently limit exploration. While AI transforms how chemists work, the expanded research capacity drives growth in total demand for chemical expertise.

title: Childcare workers
rationale: Childcare work is fundamentally physical and interpersonal, requiring real-time supervision, emotional support, diaper changing, feeding, and safety monitoring that AI cannot perform. While AI might assist with administrative tasks like record-keeping or curriculum planning, the core work of caring for children requires human presence, judgment, and emotional intelligence. Demand elasticity is high because childcare costs currently suppress demand - many parents would use more childcare services if they were more affordable, and demographic trends show persistent need. However, with disruption at only 3, this occupation falls into the resilient category as AI cannot meaningfully reshape the core caregiving responsibilities.

title: Chiropractors
rationale: Chiropractic work is fundamentally physical and hands-on, requiring manual spinal adjustments, physical examinations, and direct patient interaction that AI cannot replicate. While AI might assist with administrative tasks, patient records, or diagnostic imaging analysis, the core therapeutic work remains entirely dependent on human touch, dexterity, and real-time physical assessment. Demand elasticity is moderate because while there's growing acceptance of chiropractic care and an aging population creating more need, the personal nature of the service and regulatory constraints limit how much demand could expand even with cost reductions. The low disruption score places this firmly in the resilient category, as AI touches only peripheral aspects of the work.

title: Civil engineering technologists and technicians
rationale: Civil engineering technologists and technicians face moderate disruption as AI can assist with CAD software, data analysis, and report writing, but their core work requires physical site visits, material testing, construction observation, and hands-on technical skills that AI cannot replace. Demand elasticity is low because infrastructure projects are typically driven by regulatory requirements, safety needs, and government budgets rather than cost considerations - making infrastructure 10x cheaper wouldn't lead to 10x more bridges or roads being built. The disruption score of 6 places this occupation in the resilient category, where AI enhances some tasks but doesn't fundamentally reshape the core work requirements.

title: Civil engineers
rationale: Civil engineering involves substantial knowledge work including design, analysis, planning, and project management that AI can meaningfully assist with through CAD automation, structural analysis, cost estimation, and regulatory compliance checking. However, the core work requires physical site presence, safety oversight, regulatory approval processes, and complex stakeholder management that keeps disruption moderate. Demand elasticity is high because cheaper and faster engineering services would unlock significant infrastructure investment - more bridges, roads, and buildings would be economically viable if design and planning costs dropped substantially, especially given the massive infrastructure needs in developing regions and aging systems in developed countries.

title: Claims adjusters, appraisers, examiners, and investigators
rationale: This occupation involves predominantly knowledge work - analyzing documents, evaluating claims, calculating settlements, and making determinations based on data and evidence. AI tools can already process photographs to estimate damage, analyze medical records for necessity determinations, detect fraud patterns, and automate much of the documentation and calculation work that forms the core of these roles. Demand for insurance claims processing is highly inelastic - the number of claims is driven by accidents, natural disasters, and policy coverage, not by the cost or speed of processing. Making claims processing 10x faster doesn't create 10x more claims; it simply means fewer adjusters are needed to handle the same volume of work.

title: Clinical laboratory technologists and technicians
rationale: Clinical lab work involves significant physical specimen handling, equipment operation, and quality control procedures that require human oversight, though AI is increasingly automating analysis and pattern recognition in areas like cytology and molecular diagnostics. Demand for lab testing is relatively inelastic - it's driven by medical necessity rather than cost, and patients don't get dramatically more tests just because they're cheaper or faster to process. The moderate disruption score reflects AI's growing role in automated analysis, but the low elasticity and physical requirements keep this in the resilient category rather than restructure.

title: Coaches and scouts
rationale: Coaching and scouting remain fundamentally human-centered activities requiring physical presence, real-time interpersonal interaction, and complex situational judgment that AI cannot replicate. While AI tools can assist with video analysis, performance tracking, and data-driven insights about player statistics, the core work of motivating athletes, making split-second strategic decisions during games, and building team chemistry requires human emotional intelligence and physical presence. Demand elasticity is high because cheaper analytical tools and performance insights could expand access to quality coaching at youth and amateur levels, but the disruption score remains moderate since the essential human elements of leadership, motivation, and real-time decision-making cannot be automated.

title: Compensation and benefits managers
rationale: Compensation and benefits managers perform predominantly analytical and administrative tasks that are highly susceptible to AI automation - analyzing wage data, comparing compensation structures, managing vendor relationships, and ensuring regulatory compliance are all data-driven activities that AI tools can increasingly handle. The work involves substantial use of analytical software, database management, and report generation that AI can streamline or automate. However, demand elasticity is low because organizations have relatively fixed needs for compensation management regardless of cost - they don't suddenly need 10x more pay structure analysis or benefits administration just because AI makes it cheaper, and regulatory requirements create structural constraints on demand expansion.

title: Compliance officers
rationale: Compliance work is predominantly knowledge-based involving document review, policy analysis, regulatory interpretation, and report writing - all tasks where AI tools are already proving highly capable. AI can rapidly analyze regulations, identify compliance gaps, generate policies, and produce audit reports. However, demand elasticity is low because compliance requirements are typically fixed by regulation rather than driven by cost considerations - organizations need exactly the level of compliance required by law, regardless of how cheaply it can be delivered. While some expansion might occur as smaller organizations access compliance services, this is constrained by the fundamentally regulatory nature of the work.

title: Computer and information systems managers
rationale: IT managers face high disruption as AI transforms core activities like system analysis, resource planning, vendor evaluation, and strategic decision-making through automated insights and recommendations. However, demand elasticity is high because cheaper, AI-enhanced IT management enables organizations to pursue more ambitious digital transformation projects, expand IT infrastructure faster, and justify previously cost-prohibitive technology initiatives. The 15% projected job growth despite AI advancement suggests demand expansion will absorb productivity gains, making this an augmentation scenario where managers leverage AI tools to oversee larger, more complex technology portfolios.

title: Computer hardware engineers
rationale: Computer hardware engineers face high disruption as AI increasingly automates design optimization, circuit analysis, testing protocols, and even generates hardware schematics - core engineering tasks that are becoming digitized and AI-assisted. However, demand elasticity is high because cheaper, faster hardware design enables exponential growth in IoT devices, edge computing, autonomous systems, and AI chips themselves - creating massive latent demand for custom hardware solutions. The proliferation of smart devices across industries from automotive to healthcare represents near-unlimited demand currently constrained by design costs and time-to-market.

title: Computer network architects
rationale: Computer network architects face high disruption as AI increasingly automates network design, optimization, configuration management, and troubleshooting tasks that form their core work. AI tools can already generate network topologies, predict performance bottlenecks, and automate deployment processes. However, demand elasticity is high because cheaper and faster network design unlocks significant latent demand - more organizations can afford sophisticated network architectures, cloud migrations accelerate, and AI/IoT expansion requires extensive new network infrastructure. The productivity gains from AI-assisted design enable architects to handle more complex projects and serve previously underserved market segments.

title: Computer programmers
rationale: Computer programming scores very high on disruption (9) because the core work—writing, testing, and debugging code—is entirely digital and cognitive, directly in AI's path. AI coding tools like GitHub Copilot, ChatGPT, and specialized programming assistants already materially change how programmers work, dramatically accelerating code generation, debugging, and documentation. Elasticity scores high (8) because cheaper and faster programming unlocks significant latent demand—more businesses can afford custom software, more ideas become economically viable to build, and the barrier to creating digital solutions drops substantially. While individual programmer productivity may increase 10x, the expanded demand for software across all sectors means the occupation augments rather than contracts.

title: Computer support specialists
rationale: Computer support work is predominantly digital and cognitive, involving troubleshooting, documentation, and user guidance - tasks that AI chatbots, automated diagnostic tools, and self-service systems are already handling effectively. The BLS projects a 3% decline specifically citing automation through chatbots replacing user support functions. Demand elasticity is low because organizations have relatively fixed IT support needs regardless of cost - they don't suddenly need 10x more help desk tickets resolved just because it becomes cheaper, and many are actively seeking to reduce support volume through better systems and automation.

title: Computer systems analysts
rationale: Computer systems analysts face high disruption as AI can automate much of their core work including system analysis, requirements gathering, cost-benefit analysis, and even some system design and testing tasks. However, demand elasticity is high because cheaper and faster system analysis will unlock significant latent demand - more organizations will be able to afford comprehensive IT assessments, smaller companies will access enterprise-level analysis, and the rapid pace of technological change (including AI adoption itself) creates continuous need for system optimization. The net effect is augmentation as productivity gains enable analysts to serve more clients and tackle more complex projects rather than simply displacing workers.

title: Conservation scientists and foresters
rationale: Conservation scientists and foresters have moderate disruption as AI assists with data analysis, GIS mapping, and forest monitoring through satellite imagery and drones, but the core work remains heavily field-based requiring physical presence for site assessment, fire suppression, and hands-on land management. Elasticity is moderate because while AI-enhanced monitoring and analysis could make conservation work more efficient and potentially expand coverage of protected lands, demand is constrained by government budgets, regulatory frameworks, and the finite nature of land resources. Since disruption is moderate (4), this occupation falls into the resilient category where AI enhances but doesn't fundamentally reshape the core work.

title: Construction managers
rationale: Construction managers have moderate disruption as AI can assist with cost estimation, scheduling, planning software, and administrative tasks, but the core work requires extensive on-site presence, real-time problem-solving, coordination with physical trades, and human judgment for safety and quality issues. Demand elasticity is high because cheaper and more efficient project management could unlock more construction projects, especially smaller ones that previously couldn't afford professional management, and enable managers to oversee more concurrent projects. However, since disruption remains moderate at 5 (below the 7 threshold), this occupation falls into the resilient category despite high elasticity.

title: Cost estimators
rationale: Cost estimation is predominantly analytical work involving data processing, calculations, and pattern recognition from historical projects - core capabilities where AI excels and is already making significant inroads through specialized software. The work involves reading technical documents, analyzing databases, performing calculations, and generating reports, all of which are highly susceptible to AI automation. Demand elasticity is low because cost estimation is typically a fixed input per project - businesses don't suddenly need 10x more estimates just because they're cheaper to produce, and the BLS already projects -4% employment decline due to productivity improvements from estimation software.

title: Court reporters and simultaneous captioners
rationale: AI speech-to-text technology has advanced dramatically and can now transcribe spoken dialogue with high accuracy in real-time, directly targeting the core function of court reporters and captioners. Modern AI systems can handle multiple speakers, legal terminology, and produce formatted transcripts - capabilities that were the exclusive domain of human stenographers just years ago. Demand elasticity is low because legal proceedings require a fixed number of transcripts regardless of cost, and accessibility requirements for captioning are largely regulatory-driven rather than price-sensitive. The combination of high AI disruption to core transcription tasks and inelastic demand for these services positions this occupation for significant headcount reduction as AI tools become more reliable and widely adopted.

title: Customer service representatives
rationale: Customer service work is predominantly digital and language-based, involving phone calls, chat, email, and computer systems - all areas where AI chatbots and automated systems are already demonstrating strong capabilities and rapidly improving. The BLS already projects a 5% decline due to automation of simple tasks, and AI will accelerate this trend to handle increasingly complex inquiries. Demand elasticity is very low because customer service is typically a cost center rather than a revenue driver - companies want to minimize these costs, not expand service volume, so productivity gains from AI will directly translate to fewer human representatives needed rather than more service being provided.

title: Data scientists
rationale: Data science work is predominantly digital and cognitive - analyzing data, building models, coding algorithms, and creating visualizations - all tasks that AI tools are rapidly advancing in and already materially assist with today. The demand elasticity is high because cheaper and faster data analysis unlocks significant latent demand across industries; as AI makes data science more accessible and cost-effective, more organizations will pursue data-driven insights they previously couldn't afford, and existing data science teams will tackle broader and deeper analytical challenges. While AI will dramatically change how data scientists work, the expanding demand for data-driven decision making will likely absorb productivity gains and maintain or grow employment in this field.

title: Database administrators and architects
rationale: Database administration and architecture involves highly digital, cognitive work that AI can significantly impact - from automated database optimization and query generation to AI-powered monitoring and security management. Many core tasks like performance tuning, backup scheduling, and basic troubleshooting are already being automated by AI tools. However, demand elasticity is moderate because while cheaper database management could enable more organizations to implement sophisticated data systems, growth is constrained by the finite number of organizations needing databases and regulatory/security requirements that maintain human oversight needs. The occupation will survive but require substantial skill shifts toward AI tool management, complex architecture design, and strategic data governance.

title: Delivery truck drivers and driver/sales workers
rationale: The disruption score is moderate because while AI can optimize routes and handle some administrative tasks like scheduling and tracking, the core work remains fundamentally physical - driving trucks, loading/unloading cargo, and face-to-face customer interactions in unpredictable real-world environments. Autonomous vehicles for local delivery are still years away from widespread deployment. The elasticity is moderate-to-high because cheaper and faster delivery could unlock significant latent demand for e-commerce and local delivery services, but growth is somewhat constrained by existing infrastructure and regulatory frameworks. Since disruption is moderate (4), this occupation falls into the resilient category regardless of elasticity.

title: Dental assistants
rationale: Dental assistants' core work is predominantly physical and hands-on - sterilizing instruments, positioning patients, holding suction equipment, taking x-rays with physical positioning, and direct patient care. While AI might help with some administrative tasks like scheduling and record-keeping, the majority of their duties require physical presence and manual dexterity in a clinical setting. Demand for dental assistant services is relatively inelastic because it's tied directly to the number of dental procedures performed, which is constrained by patient needs, insurance coverage, and regulatory requirements rather than cost. Even if AI made some tasks more efficient, dental offices would still need the same number of assistants to physically support procedures and patient care.

title: Dentists
rationale: Dentistry involves predominantly hands-on physical procedures requiring manual dexterity, direct patient interaction, and real-time clinical judgment in unpredictable biological environments. While AI assists with diagnostic imaging analysis and practice management, the core work of drilling, filling, extracting teeth, and performing oral surgery remains fundamentally manual and requires human presence. Demand elasticity is moderate because cheaper/faster dental care could unlock some latent demand (more people getting preventive care, cosmetic procedures), but is constrained by the physical time required per patient, regulatory requirements, and the fact that people have limited teeth and oral health needs. The disruption score of 4 keeps this in the resilient category despite moderate elasticity.

title: Dietitians and nutritionists
rationale: AI significantly disrupts the knowledge-work portions of dietetics - meal planning, nutritional analysis, research interpretation, and educational material creation can be enhanced by AI tools, but the core human elements of counseling, empathy, and personalized care remain essential. Demand elasticity is high because nutrition services are currently expensive and inaccessible to many; AI-powered tools could dramatically reduce costs and enable personalized nutrition guidance at scale, unlocking massive latent demand from individuals who cannot currently afford dietitian services. The combination of meaningful AI enhancement with strong demand expansion suggests workers will produce dramatically more value per person rather than being displaced.

title: Drywall installers, ceiling tile installers, and tapers
rationale: This occupation involves predominantly physical, hands-on work in unpredictable construction environments - measuring, cutting, lifting heavy panels, working at heights, and applying materials with precise manual dexterity. AI has minimal relevance to these core physical tasks, though it might assist with peripheral functions like material estimation or project planning. Demand elasticity is low because construction projects have fixed requirements for drywall installation regardless of cost - buildings need a specific amount of drywall coverage that doesn't expand significantly even if installation became cheaper. The physical, site-specific nature of the work makes this occupation highly resilient to AI disruption.

title: Electrical and electronics engineers
rationale: Electrical and electronics engineers face high disruption as AI increasingly automates design optimization, circuit analysis, simulation, and testing processes that form core parts of their work. However, demand elasticity is high because cheaper and faster engineering enables more electronic products, systems, and infrastructure projects that were previously cost-prohibitive. The growing complexity of AI-powered devices, IoT systems, renewable energy infrastructure, and autonomous technologies creates expanding demand for electrical engineering expertise. While AI transforms how the work is done, the net effect is augmentation as engineers become more productive and tackle increasingly sophisticated projects.

title: Electricians
rationale: Electricians score low on disruption because their core work is predominantly physical and hands-on - installing wiring, connecting fixtures, troubleshooting electrical problems in real-world environments that require manual dexterity, physical presence, and safety expertise that AI cannot replicate. While AI might assist with peripheral tasks like reading blueprints or diagnostic analysis, the fundamental work of physically manipulating electrical systems remains unchanged. Elasticity is moderate because cheaper electrical services could unlock some latent demand (more buildings getting upgraded electrical systems, expanded renewable energy installations), but demand is somewhat constrained by building codes, safety regulations, and the physical reality that most buildings already have electrical systems. The resilient classification fits because AI disruption remains minimal despite moderate demand elasticity potential.

title: Elementary, middle, and high school principals
rationale: School principals face moderate disruption as AI can assist with data analysis, scheduling, budget management, and report generation, but the core work remains deeply interpersonal - managing staff relationships, disciplining students, community engagement, and crisis management require human judgment and presence. Demand elasticity is very low because the number of principal positions is structurally fixed by the number of schools, which is determined by student enrollment and district budgets rather than productivity gains. Even if AI makes principals more efficient at administrative tasks, schools still need exactly one principal each, making this occupation resilient to AI-driven displacement.

title: EMTs and paramedics
rationale: EMTs and paramedics perform predominantly physical, hands-on emergency medical care requiring real-time assessment, manual procedures, patient transport, and human presence in unpredictable emergency situations. While AI might assist with some documentation or diagnostic support, the core work of physically responding to emergencies, performing life-saving procedures, and providing human care remains largely unchanged. Demand for emergency medical services is relatively inelastic - the number of medical emergencies, accidents, and health crises doesn't significantly increase just because response becomes more efficient. The work remains fundamentally resilient to AI disruption due to its physical, interpersonal, and real-world emergency response nature.

title: Environmental science and protection technicians
rationale: The disruption score is moderate because while AI can assist with data analysis, report generation, and pattern recognition in environmental monitoring, the core work requires physical fieldwork, sample collection, equipment maintenance, and on-site inspections that cannot be automated. The elasticity is moderate-to-high because cheaper and faster environmental analysis could unlock demand from smaller businesses and enable more comprehensive monitoring, but regulatory frameworks and budget constraints limit expansion. Since disruption remains at the moderate level (5), this occupation falls into the resilient category despite moderate demand elasticity.

title: Epidemiologists
rationale: Epidemiologists face high disruption as their core work involves data analysis, statistical modeling, pattern recognition, and research synthesis - all areas where AI tools are already proving highly valuable for processing large datasets, identifying disease patterns, and generating insights. However, demand elasticity is high because cheaper, faster epidemiological analysis could dramatically expand public health surveillance, enable real-time disease monitoring in underserved regions, support personalized health interventions, and allow for more comprehensive population health studies that are currently cost-prohibitive. The growing complexity of global health challenges and the increasing availability of health data create substantial latent demand for epidemiological expertise that AI-enhanced productivity could unlock.

title: Farmers, ranchers, and other agricultural managers
rationale: Agricultural management involves substantial physical oversight of crops, livestock, and land that requires real-world presence and hands-on decision-making based on local conditions, weather, and biological systems. While AI can assist with data analysis, market monitoring, and some planning tasks, the core work remains deeply rooted in physical agricultural operations and direct management of biological systems. Demand for agricultural products is relatively inelastic - people don't consume dramatically more food if it becomes cheaper, and regulatory/land constraints limit expansion. The moderate disruption combined with structural demand limitations places this occupation in the resilient category, where AI enhances peripheral tasks but doesn't fundamentally reshape the core work.

title: Financial clerks
rationale: Financial clerks perform predominantly digital, routine tasks like data entry, record keeping, calculations, and transaction processing that AI can already handle well or will soon master. The work involves structured financial data manipulation with clear rules and procedures, making it highly susceptible to automation. Demand for financial clerk services is largely inelastic - businesses and individuals need a fixed amount of billing, payroll processing, and account management regardless of cost, and regulatory requirements often mandate specific processes that don't expand with efficiency gains. The BLS already projects a 5% decline, indicating that productivity improvements from AI will translate directly to fewer workers needed rather than expanded service demand.

title: Fishing and hunting workers
rationale: Fishing and hunting work is almost entirely physical and hands-on, requiring real-world presence in unpredictable marine and wilderness environments. While AI might assist with fish-finding equipment or navigation, the core tasks of operating boats, handling nets, processing catches, and working in harsh conditions remain fundamentally manual. Demand for fish and game is relatively fixed by consumption patterns and regulatory quotas, with productivity gains unlikely to expand total market demand significantly. The occupation faces resilience to AI disruption due to its physical nature and environmental unpredictability.

title: Food and beverage serving and related workers
rationale: Food service work is predominantly physical and interpersonal - taking orders face-to-face, carrying trays, cleaning tables, and providing in-person customer service - with AI only helping with peripheral tasks like digital ordering systems. While AI could automate some order-taking and payment processing, the core work requires human presence for food handling, customer interaction, and real-world coordination. Demand shows moderate elasticity as cheaper/faster service could expand dining out and food delivery markets, but the low disruption score of 3 places this firmly in the resilient category where AI touches the periphery but doesn't reshape core work.

title: Forest and conservation workers
rationale: Forest and conservation workers perform predominantly physical, hands-on tasks in outdoor environments - planting seedlings, clearing brush, operating power tools, fighting fires, and maintaining trails. While some administrative tasks like tree counting might benefit from remote sensing technology, the core work remains manual and requires human presence in unpredictable natural settings. Demand for forest conservation services is relatively inelastic, constrained by fixed forest acreage, government budgets, and regulatory frameworks rather than cost considerations. Even if productivity improved dramatically, the total amount of forest land requiring maintenance and the frequency of conservation activities would not expand proportionally.

title: Funeral service workers
rationale: Funeral service work involves substantial physical presence, emotional counseling, and hands-on preparation of remains - core tasks that require human empathy, cultural sensitivity, and physical manipulation that AI cannot replicate. While AI might assist with administrative tasks like filing death certificates or scheduling, the essential work of comforting grieving families, preparing bodies, and managing funeral logistics requires human presence and emotional intelligence. Demand for funeral services is highly inelastic as it's driven by death rates rather than cost considerations - people don't consume more funeral services when they become cheaper, and the number of deaths in a population is relatively fixed. The low disruption and inelastic demand clearly place this occupation in the resilient category.

title: Genetic counselors
rationale: AI significantly disrupts genetic counseling by automating risk assessment calculations, pattern recognition in genetic data, and generating initial interpretations of test results, but the core human elements of empathetic counseling, complex family dynamics navigation, and personalized decision support remain essential. Demand elasticity is high because genetic counseling is currently severely supply-constrained with only 4,000 practitioners nationwide - AI tools that make counselors more efficient could dramatically expand access to genetic services for the millions who currently lack it, especially as genetic testing becomes more common and affordable. The combination of meaningful AI assistance with substantial unmet demand positions this as an augmentation scenario where productivity gains enable serving many more patients rather than reducing headcount.

title: Geographers
rationale: Geographers' core work involves data analysis, GIS programming, mapping, and research synthesis - predominantly digital knowledge work that AI can increasingly automate, including satellite image analysis, pattern recognition in geographic data, and report generation. Demand elasticity is low because geographic analysis needs are largely driven by fixed government planning requirements, regulatory compliance, and infrastructure projects rather than cost considerations - making 10x cheaper analysis unlikely to generate proportionally more demand. The combination of high AI disruption potential in data-intensive analytical work and constrained demand growth suggests significant headcount contraction is likely.

title: Geological and hydrologic technicians
rationale: The disruption score is moderate because while AI can assist with data analysis, report writing, and pattern recognition in geological samples, the core work requires significant fieldwork including sample collection, equipment installation and maintenance, and physical presence in remote locations. The elasticity is low because demand for geological and hydrologic services is largely driven by regulatory requirements, infrastructure needs, and natural resource extraction - areas where cheaper analysis doesn't dramatically expand total demand. Since disruption is moderate (5) rather than high (7+), this occupation falls into the resilient category where AI enhances some analytical tasks but doesn't fundamentally reshape the core work.

title: Geoscientists
rationale: Geoscientists face moderate disruption as AI can assist with data analysis, pattern recognition in geological data, and report writing, but the core work requires extensive fieldwork, physical sample collection, and real-world presence that AI cannot replace. The occupation involves substantial physical components like hiking to remote locations, drilling, and hands-on laboratory work. Demand elasticity is low because geological surveys and resource assessments are typically driven by specific project needs rather than cost considerations - making geological work 10x cheaper wouldn't dramatically increase the number of oil wells drilled or environmental assessments conducted. The combination of moderate disruption and low elasticity places this in the resilient category, where AI enhances certain analytical tasks but doesn't fundamentally reshape the occupation.

title: Graphic designers
rationale: Graphic design scores high on disruption because AI tools like DALL-E, Midjourney, and Canva's AI features already materially change how visual content is created, from generating initial concepts to automating layout decisions and producing finished designs. The work is predominantly digital and cognitive, making it directly in AI's path. Elasticity is moderate because while cheaper design could unlock demand from small businesses and individuals who previously couldn't afford professional design services, the market faces structural constraints from established brand relationships, regulatory requirements for certain designs, and the finite attention span for visual content consumption. This combination suggests the occupation will survive but require substantial skill adaptation toward creative direction, client strategy, and AI tool mastery rather than manual design execution.

title: Grounds maintenance workers
rationale: Grounds maintenance work is overwhelmingly physical and hands-on, requiring real-world presence for tasks like mowing, planting, pruning, and equipment operation in unpredictable outdoor environments. While AI might assist with peripheral tasks like scheduling or plant identification, the core work of physical landscape maintenance remains largely untouched by current AI capabilities. Demand shows moderate elasticity as cheaper maintenance services could unlock more frequent care for existing properties and enable maintenance of previously neglected spaces, but the low disruption score keeps this occupation in the resilient category where AI touches only the periphery of the work.

title: Health information technologists and medical registrars
