While AI might assist with peripheral tasks like scheduling or basic patient education materials, the core work of cleaning teeth, taking x-rays, and providing personalized oral health guidance remains fundamentally manual and interpersonal. Demand elasticity is constrained by regulatory requirements, the need for physical presence, and the fact that people don't dramatically increase dental cleanings even if they become cheaper. The low disruption score keeps this occupation in the resilient category despite moderate elasticity limitations.

title: Desktop publishers
rationale: Desktop publishing is almost entirely digital work involving layout design, text formatting, and graphic integration - tasks that AI tools like advanced design software and automated layout systems can increasingly handle. Modern AI can already generate layouts, format text, and integrate graphics with minimal human oversight. Demand for desktop publishing services is highly inelastic because organizations have fixed publication needs and won't dramatically increase their publishing volume even if costs drop significantly. The BLS already projects a 12% decline, reflecting how these tasks are being absorbed by other roles and automated systems.

title: Diagnostic medical sonographers
rationale: AI is moderately disrupting sonography through automated image analysis, quality assessment, and preliminary diagnostic assistance, but the core work still requires physical patient interaction, equipment operation, and real-time decision-making about positioning and image acquisition. Demand elasticity is high because cheaper, faster ultrasound analysis could dramatically expand access to diagnostic imaging, particularly in underserved areas, rural locations, and for preventive screening programs where cost currently limits utilization. The combination of moderate disruption with high demand expansion suggests sonographers will benefit from AI augmentation rather than replacement, as productivity gains enable broader healthcare access.

title: Diesel service technicians and mechanics
rationale: Diesel service technicians work primarily with physical, hands-on repair of mechanical systems, though modern diagnostic tools and electronic engine components introduce some digital elements. While AI-powered diagnostic systems may assist with troubleshooting, the core work remains manual - physically inspecting, disassembling, repairing, and reassembling heavy mechanical components that require human dexterity, strength, and real-world problem-solving. Demand elasticity is low because the need for diesel engine maintenance is driven by the existing fleet of vehicles and equipment, not by cost considerations - trucks and buses need repair regardless of labor costs, and there's limited scope for dramatically increased maintenance frequency even if it became cheaper.

title: Drafters
rationale: Drafters work almost entirely in digital environments using CAD and BIM software, making their core tasks highly susceptible to AI automation. AI tools are already emerging that can generate technical drawings from specifications and convert rough sketches into detailed plans. Demand for drafting services is relatively inelastic because the need for technical drawings is tied to specific construction and manufacturing projects - making drawings 10x cheaper wouldn't lead to 10x more buildings or products being designed. The BLS already projects flat to declining employment (-6% to -7% for most specialties) as CAD/BIM technologies increase productivity, and AI will accelerate this trend.

title: Economists
rationale: Economists' core work—data analysis, statistical modeling, forecasting, and research—is highly digital and cognitive, making it prime for AI disruption. AI tools already assist with data processing, pattern recognition, and basic economic modeling, with rapid advancement in these capabilities. Demand elasticity is moderate because while cheaper economic analysis could expand access for smaller businesses and organizations, the field is constrained by the specialized nature of economic expertise and regulatory/institutional factors that limit how much economic analysis is actually consumed. The combination of high disruption and moderate elasticity suggests the occupation will survive but require substantial skill adaptation toward higher-level interpretation, policy judgment, and complex problem-solving that AI cannot yet handle.

title: Editors
rationale: Editors face very high disruption as AI can already perform many core tasks like grammar correction, fact-checking, content generation, and basic rewriting - all primarily digital, language-based work that's in AI's direct path. Demand elasticity is moderate because while cheaper editing could unlock some latent demand (more content creation, better quality for smaller publications), the market is constrained by finite reader attention, established publication cycles, and the fact that much editorial work is tied to fixed content volumes rather than cost-sensitive demand. The combination of high disruption and moderate elasticity suggests the occupation will survive but require substantial skill shifts toward higher-level creative direction, strategic content planning, and human judgment tasks that complement AI capabilities.

title: Electrical and electronic engineering technologists and technicians
rationale: While AI can assist with design analysis, documentation, and some diagnostic tasks, the core work involves hands-on assembly, calibration, testing, and repair of physical electrical systems that require manual dexterity and real-world problem-solving. The occupation sits at the boundary between knowledge work and skilled trades, with significant physical components that AI cannot directly perform. Demand elasticity is moderate - cheaper electrical engineering support could enable more product development and system maintenance, but growth is constrained by the physical nature of electrical systems and infrastructure needs. The disruption score of 6 places this just at the resilient threshold, where AI augments but doesn't fundamentally reshape the core work.

title: Electrical and electronics installers and repairers
rationale: This occupation scores low on disruption because the core work is predominantly physical and hands-on - diagnosing equipment problems through physical inspection, disassembling machinery, replacing components, and reassembling systems in real-world industrial environments. While AI might assist with some diagnostic software or record-keeping, the fundamental tasks require manual dexterity, physical presence at equipment locations, and tactile problem-solving that AI cannot replicate. Elasticity is low because demand for electrical repairs is largely driven by equipment failure rates and maintenance schedules rather than cost - making repairs 10x cheaper wouldn't significantly increase the number of motors that break down or substations that need maintenance. The resilient classification fits because AI disruption remains minimal to the core physical repair work.

title: Electrical power-line installers and repairers
rationale: This occupation scores low on disruption because the core work is predominantly physical and hands-on - climbing poles, installing heavy cables, working at heights, and performing manual repairs in unpredictable outdoor environments that AI cannot replicate. While AI might assist with diagnostic equipment or route planning, the fundamental tasks require human presence, physical strength, and real-world problem-solving. Elasticity is low-moderate because demand for electrical infrastructure is relatively inelastic - the grid needs what it needs based on population and development, and even if maintenance became more efficient, utilities wouldn't dramatically expand beyond necessary infrastructure. The resilient category fits because AI disruption remains minimal for this physically-demanding, location-specific occupation.

title: Electro-mechanical and mechatronics technologists and technicians
rationale: The disruption score is moderate because while AI can assist with some analytical tasks like test result analysis, documentation, and troubleshooting guidance, the core work remains heavily physical and hands-on - operating machinery, soldering circuits, calibrating equipment, and maintaining robotic systems in industrial environments. The elasticity score reflects that cheaper automation and better maintenance could drive increased demand for automated systems across industries, but this growth is constrained by capital investment cycles and regulatory requirements in manufacturing. Since disruption is moderate (5) and below the threshold of 7, this occupation falls into the resilient category despite moderate demand elasticity.

title: Elevator and escalator installers and repairers
rationale: This occupation involves predominantly physical, hands-on work in unpredictable real-world environments - installing heavy mechanical equipment, working in cramped elevator shafts, troubleshooting complex electro-mechanical systems, and performing emergency repairs. While AI might assist with some diagnostic tasks or reading blueprints, the core work requires physical presence, manual dexterity, and real-world problem-solving that AI cannot replicate. Demand elasticity is low because elevator/escalator needs are tied to fixed building infrastructure - making installation 10x cheaper wouldn't lead people to install significantly more elevators, as demand is constrained by building construction and regulatory requirements rather than cost.

title: Emergency management directors
rationale: Emergency management directors have moderate disruption potential as AI can assist with data analysis, risk assessment modeling, and communication planning, but the core work requires human judgment in crisis situations, coordination with multiple stakeholders, and physical presence during emergencies. Demand elasticity is low because emergency management needs are largely fixed by population size, geographic risks, and regulatory requirements - making services 10x faster/cheaper wouldn't significantly increase the number of directors needed. The combination of moderate disruption and low elasticity, along with the critical human elements of leadership and real-time decision-making, places this occupation in the resilient category.

title: Entertainment and recreation managers
rationale: Entertainment and recreation managers have moderate disruption as AI can assist with scheduling, budgeting, customer feedback analysis, and staff management tasks, but the core work requires physical presence, interpersonal skills, and real-time problem-solving in dynamic environments with customers and staff. Demand elasticity is moderate because while AI-enhanced efficiency could enable better programming and customer experiences that might attract more participants, the demand is somewhat constrained by physical facility capacity, local population, and leisure spending patterns. Since disruption is moderate (5) rather than high (7+), this occupation falls into the resilient category where AI touches peripheral tasks but doesn't fundamentally reshape the core management and interpersonal aspects of the work.

title: Environmental engineering technologists and technicians
rationale: The disruption score is 6 because while AI can assist with data analysis, sample testing interpretation, and documentation tasks, the core work requires physical field sampling, equipment operation, hazardous material handling, and on-site inspections that demand human presence and judgment. The elasticity score is 4 because environmental compliance and monitoring demand is largely driven by regulatory requirements rather than cost considerations - companies and agencies must meet environmental standards regardless of testing costs, limiting demand expansion even if AI makes analysis cheaper. The net effect is resilient because the moderate disruption level (6) places this occupation in the category where AI touches peripheral tasks but doesn't fundamentally reshape the core hands-on environmental work.

title: Environmental engineers
rationale: Environmental engineers face moderate-to-high disruption as AI can assist with data analysis, report generation, regulatory compliance monitoring, and system design optimization, but core work still requires field inspections, stakeholder coordination, and regulatory expertise that demand human judgment. Demand elasticity is high because environmental challenges are growing globally while current solutions are often limited by cost and engineering capacity - cheaper AI-assisted environmental engineering could unlock massive latent demand for pollution control, remediation projects, and sustainability solutions across industries and developing regions. The combination of meaningful AI assistance with strong demand expansion positions this as an augment occupation where workers become more productive rather than displaced.

title: Environmental scientists and specialists
rationale: Environmental scientists face moderate-to-high disruption as AI can assist with data analysis, pattern recognition in environmental datasets, modeling climate impacts, and report generation, but core work still requires field sampling, regulatory compliance expertise, and human judgment for complex environmental assessments. Demand elasticity is high because cheaper and faster environmental analysis could unlock significant latent demand - more businesses could afford comprehensive environmental assessments, more sites could be monitored continuously, and more detailed climate modeling could be conducted as costs drop. The combination of meaningful AI assistance with strong demand expansion potential places this in the augment category, where productivity gains drive increased output rather than job losses.

title: Exercise physiologists
rationale: Exercise physiologists blend knowledge work (analyzing medical data, designing programs) with essential hands-on client interaction, physical assessments, and real-time monitoring that requires human presence and judgment. While AI could assist with data analysis and program design, the core work involves direct patient care, physical testing, and personalized coaching that demands human expertise and empathy. Demand elasticity is high because cheaper, more accessible exercise physiology services could expand into underserved populations and preventive care markets, but the moderate disruption score keeps this in the resilient category where AI enhances rather than transforms the occupation.

title: Fashion designers
rationale: Fashion design involves substantial digital and cognitive work including CAD programs, trend analysis, and creative conceptualization that AI tools are increasingly capable of assisting with or automating. AI can already generate design concepts, predict trends from data, and create digital prototypes, representing high disruption to core tasks. Demand elasticity is moderate - while cheaper design tools could enable more small brands and personalized fashion, the industry is still constrained by manufacturing capacity, retail channels, and consumer purchasing power rather than just design costs. The combination of high disruption and moderate elasticity suggests the occupation will survive but require significant skill adaptation toward AI collaboration and higher-level creative direction.

title: Film and video editors and camera operators
rationale: AI is highly disruptive to film and video editing through automated cutting, color correction, effects generation, and even AI-generated footage, while camera work faces moderate disruption from automated systems and drone technology. However, demand elasticity is high because cheaper, faster video production dramatically expands content creation across social media, marketing, education, and entertainment - the cost barrier has historically limited video content production. The combination of high disruption with high elasticity suggests workers will need to adapt their skills but total employment can grow as the market expands significantly.

title: Financial analysts
rationale: Financial analysts face very high disruption as their core tasks—evaluating financial data, analyzing trends, creating reports, and making investment recommendations—are predominantly digital and cognitive work that AI tools can already assist with significantly. AI can process vast amounts of financial data, identify patterns, and generate analytical reports much faster than humans. However, elasticity is moderate because while cheaper analysis could unlock some latent demand (more small businesses getting professional financial analysis, more assets being actively managed), the total demand is still constrained by regulatory requirements, the finite pool of investable assets, and institutional structures that limit how much additional analysis translates to new positions.

title: Financial examiners
rationale: Financial examination involves predominantly analytical and document review tasks that AI can increasingly handle - analyzing balance sheets, evaluating loan risk, reviewing compliance documentation, and generating reports. While some on-site inspection and human judgment remain important, the core analytical work is highly susceptible to AI automation. Demand elasticity is low because financial examination is primarily driven by regulatory requirements rather than market forces - banks need a fixed level of examination regardless of cost, and regulatory frameworks limit how much additional examination would occur even if it became cheaper. The combination of high AI disruption potential with inelastic demand suggests significant headcount reduction as AI tools become capable of performing much of the analytical work.

title: Financial managers
rationale: Financial managers face high disruption as AI can automate much of their data analysis, financial reporting, forecasting, and risk assessment tasks - core functions that are predominantly digital and analytical. However, their strategic advisory role, relationship management with executives, and complex decision-making in uncertain environments provide some resilience. Demand elasticity is moderate because while AI-enhanced financial analysis could enable more sophisticated financial management for smaller organizations and unlock some latent demand, the number of senior financial management positions is ultimately constrained by organizational structure and regulatory requirements. The occupation will survive but require significant skill adaptation toward strategic thinking, AI tool management, and higher-level advisory capabilities.

title: Fire inspectors
rationale: Fire inspection work involves substantial physical presence requirements - visiting buildings, examining physical structures, testing equipment, and investigating fire scenes with debris analysis. While AI could assist with code research, report writing, and pattern analysis in investigations, the core work requires on-site inspection, physical evidence collection, and real-world safety assessments that AI cannot perform. Demand elasticity is low because fire safety inspections are primarily driven by regulatory requirements and building construction rates rather than cost considerations - making inspections cheaper wouldn't significantly increase the total number of buildings needing inspection. The moderate disruption and low elasticity place this firmly in the resilient category.

title: Firefighters
rationale: Firefighting is fundamentally a physical, hands-on occupation requiring real-world presence for emergency response, rescue operations, and fire suppression - areas where AI has minimal relevance to core tasks. While AI might assist with some administrative tasks like report writing or equipment tracking, the essential work of entering burning buildings, operating heavy equipment, and providing emergency medical care remains entirely human-dependent. Demand for firefighting services is highly inelastic as it's driven by emergencies and regulatory requirements rather than cost considerations - communities don't hire more firefighters simply because technology makes some aspects more efficient. The occupation remains resilient to AI disruption due to its predominantly physical nature and fixed demand structure.

title: Fitness trainers and instructors
rationale: Fitness training is fundamentally physical and interpersonal work requiring real-time observation, hands-on correction, motivation, and safety supervision that AI cannot replicate. While AI can assist with program design, nutrition guidance, and administrative tasks, the core value lies in human presence, demonstration, and personalized coaching. Demand elasticity is high as cheaper fitness services through AI-assisted trainers could expand access to personal training and specialized classes, particularly for underserved populations. However, with disruption at only moderate levels, this occupation remains resilient as the essential human elements of fitness instruction cannot be meaningfully replaced by current or near-term AI capabilities.

title: Flight attendants
rationale: Flight attendants' core responsibilities are predominantly physical and interpersonal - ensuring passenger safety, conducting emergency procedures, providing hands-on customer service, and managing real-world situations that require human presence and judgment. While AI might assist with some administrative tasks like scheduling or basic passenger information, the fundamental work of physical safety demonstrations, emergency response, and in-person passenger care cannot be digitized. Demand for flight attendants is highly inelastic because it's legally mandated by federal regulations based on aircraft capacity and flight duration, meaning productivity gains from AI tools wouldn't reduce headcount requirements. The occupation remains resilient as AI cannot meaningfully disrupt the core physical and regulatory safety functions that define this role.

title: Flooring installers and tile and stone setters
rationale: This occupation involves predominantly physical, hands-on work requiring precise manual dexterity, spatial reasoning in real-world environments, and adaptation to unique site conditions that AI cannot replicate. While AI might assist with peripheral tasks like measuring or design visualization, the core work of cutting, placing, and securing flooring materials remains entirely manual. Demand elasticity is low-to-moderate as flooring installation is tied to construction cycles and renovation needs, with some potential for increased activity if costs dropped significantly, but structural constraints limit major expansion. The minimal disruption from AI keeps this occupation in the resilient category.

title: Floral designers
rationale: Floral design is fundamentally a hands-on craft requiring physical manipulation of delicate materials, artistic sensibility for color/texture/form, and direct customer interaction - areas where AI has minimal impact. While AI might assist with peripheral tasks like inventory management or basic design suggestions, the core work of selecting, cutting, arranging, and handling fresh flowers remains entirely manual and creative. Demand for floral arrangements is relatively inelastic as it's tied to fixed occasions (weddings, funerals, holidays) and personal gifting patterns that wouldn't dramatically expand even if costs dropped significantly. The occupation remains resilient to AI disruption due to its physical, artistic, and interpersonal nature.

title: Food preparation workers
rationale: Food preparation work is predominantly physical and manual - chopping vegetables, handling meat, cleaning equipment, and maintaining food safety standards in real-world kitchen environments. While some inventory management or ordering systems may use AI, the core tasks require human dexterity, physical presence, and real-time adaptation to kitchen conditions. Demand for food preparation is relatively inelastic because it's tied to fixed meal volumes and restaurant capacity rather than cost of preparation labor. Even if preparation became more efficient, restaurants don't suddenly serve 10x more meals - they're constrained by seating, cooking capacity, and customer demand patterns.

title: Food processing equipment workers
rationale: Food processing equipment work is predominantly physical and hands-on, involving machine operation, ingredient handling, temperature monitoring, and equipment cleaning in real manufacturing environments. While some data recording tasks could be digitized, the core work requires physical presence, manual dexterity, and real-time sensory monitoring that AI cannot replicate. Demand for food processing is relatively inelastic - people don't consume dramatically more processed food if production becomes cheaper, and the market is already mature with established consumption patterns. The combination of low disruption potential and inelastic demand makes this occupation resilient to AI displacement.

title: Food service managers
rationale: Food service managers have moderate disruption because while AI can assist with scheduling, inventory management, payroll, and basic financial tracking, the core responsibilities require physical presence, interpersonal leadership, real-time problem-solving with staff and customers, and hands-on oversight of kitchen operations. The elasticity is low because demand for restaurant management is tied to the number of physical establishments and dining occasions, which don't dramatically expand even if management becomes more efficient - each restaurant still needs roughly the same management structure regardless of AI productivity gains. With disruption at 4 and elasticity at 3, this occupation falls into the resilient category where AI touches peripheral administrative tasks but doesn't reshape the fundamentally human-centered, physical management work.

title: Forensic science technicians
rationale: Forensic science technicians face moderate disruption as AI can assist with pattern recognition, DNA analysis, and report generation, but core work requires physical evidence collection, chain of custody maintenance, and expert testimony that demands human judgment and legal credibility. Demand elasticity is low because forensic analysis is driven by crime rates and legal requirements rather than cost considerations - courts don't order 10x more forensic tests if they become cheaper, and the justice system has strict protocols that limit volume expansion. The disruption score of 6 places this occupation in the resilient category, where AI enhances certain analytical tasks but doesn't fundamentally reshape the profession's core requirements.

title: Fundraisers
rationale: Fundraising involves substantial knowledge work including donor research, message crafting, campaign strategy, and digital outreach that AI can meaningfully enhance through better prospect identification, personalized messaging, and automated donor management. However, the core relationship-building, face-to-face cultivation, and trust-building aspects remain fundamentally human activities. Demand elasticity is high because cheaper, more effective fundraising tools would enable smaller nonprofits to professionalize their development efforts and allow larger organizations to expand their donor base significantly - there's substantial latent demand for fundraising services currently constrained by cost and expertise barriers.

title: Gambling services workers
rationale: Gambling services work is predominantly physical and interpersonal, requiring real-time human presence for customer interaction, game operation, and security monitoring in casino environments. While AI might assist with some administrative tasks or basic customer queries, the core functions of dealing cards, managing tables, enforcing rules, and providing personalized customer service remain fundamentally human-centered activities. Demand for gambling services is relatively inelastic as it's driven by entertainment preferences and regulatory constraints rather than cost efficiency - making gambling 10x cheaper wouldn't dramatically increase the number of people gambling or the frequency of their visits. The low disruption score combined with inelastic demand places this occupation firmly in the resilient category.

title: General maintenance and repair workers
rationale: General maintenance and repair work is predominantly physical and hands-on, involving tasks like fixing plumbing, electrical work, painting, and machinery repair that require real-world presence and manual dexterity. While AI might assist with some peripheral tasks like work order management or diagnostic guidance, the core work of physically repairing and maintaining equipment and buildings remains largely unchanged. Demand for maintenance services is relatively inelastic - buildings and equipment need a fixed amount of maintenance regardless of cost, and making repairs faster/cheaper doesn't typically generate significantly more maintenance work. The low disruption score combined with inelastic demand places this occupation firmly in the resilient category.

title: General office clerks
rationale: General office clerks perform highly routine, digital tasks like data entry, document processing, filing, and phone answering that AI and automation can already handle effectively. Most core duties involve structured information processing with minimal human judgment required. Demand for clerical work is relatively fixed - organizations need a certain amount of administrative support regardless of cost, and making it cheaper doesn't create substantially more need for filing, data entry, or basic customer service. The BLS already projects 7% employment decline, reflecting ongoing automation of these routine tasks.

title: Glaziers
rationale: Glaziers perform predominantly physical, hands-on work requiring precise measurement, cutting, lifting, and installation of glass in real-world construction environments. While AI might assist with peripheral tasks like reading blueprints or project planning, the core work of handling heavy glass, working at heights, and performing precise physical installations cannot be automated. Demand elasticity is low because glass installation needs are tied to construction projects and building maintenance, which don't dramatically expand even if installation became cheaper - buildings need a fixed amount of windows and glass fixtures regardless of labor costs.

title: Hand laborers and material movers
rationale: This occupation scores low on disruption because the core work is almost entirely physical - manually moving materials, lifting heavy objects, packing by hand, and cleaning vehicles. While some workers use handheld scanners for tracking, the fundamental tasks require human presence and physical manipulation in unpredictable real-world environments. Elasticity is low-moderate because demand is somewhat constrained by structural factors - the amount of freight to move, garbage to collect, and vehicles to clean is largely determined by economic activity levels and population, though e-commerce growth does create some additional demand for stockers and order fillers. The resilient category fits because AI cannot meaningfully change the core physical nature of this work.

title: Hazardous materials removal workers
rationale: This occupation involves predominantly physical, hands-on work in hazardous environments requiring real-world presence, specialized safety protocols, and manual removal of dangerous materials - areas where AI has minimal relevance to core tasks. While AI might assist with some peripheral activities like record-keeping or material identification, the fundamental work of physically removing asbestos, lead, radioactive materials, and other hazmat requires human workers in protective equipment performing manual labor. Demand elasticity is very low because hazmat cleanup is typically mandated by regulation, driven by specific contamination events, or required for facility decommissioning - making it largely price-insensitive with fixed demand regardless of cost improvements.

title: Health and safety engineers
rationale: Health and safety engineers face moderate disruption as AI can assist with data analysis, regulatory research, hazard pattern recognition, and report generation, but core activities like physical site inspections, accident investigations, and safety program implementation require human judgment and presence. Demand elasticity is low because safety requirements are largely driven by regulatory compliance and risk management rather than cost considerations - organizations don't dramatically increase safety engineering when it becomes cheaper, as they're already motivated by legal requirements and liability concerns. The moderate disruption level keeps this occupation in the resilient category, where AI enhances certain analytical tasks but doesn't fundamentally reshape the profession's core human-centered responsibilities.

title: Health education specialists
rationale: Health education specialists face moderate-to-high disruption as AI can assist with program development, data analysis, content creation, and personalized health messaging, but the core work still requires human interaction, community engagement, and cultural sensitivity. Demand elasticity is high because cheaper, AI-enhanced health education could dramatically expand access to personalized health guidance, workplace wellness programs, and community health initiatives that are currently limited by cost and staffing constraints. The combination of meaningful AI assistance with strong demand expansion potential makes this an augment scenario where workers become more productive rather than displaced.

title: Heavy vehicle and mobile equipment service technicians
rationale: This occupation involves predominantly hands-on mechanical work requiring physical presence, manual dexterity, and real-world problem-solving with heavy equipment that cannot be digitized. While AI may assist with diagnostic tools and maintenance scheduling, the core tasks of disassembling, repairing, and reassembling heavy machinery remain fundamentally physical and require human expertise. Demand elasticity is low-moderate because equipment maintenance needs are largely fixed by the existing fleet size and regulatory requirements, with some potential expansion from more complex equipment requiring specialized service. The low disruption score keeps this occupation in the resilient category despite moderate elasticity.

title: High school teachers
rationale: High school teaching involves substantial knowledge work - lesson planning, content delivery, assessment, and communication - that AI can meaningfully assist with through personalized learning platforms, automated grading, and curriculum development tools. However, the core human elements of classroom management, student motivation, and real-time adaptation to student needs remain essential. Demand elasticity is high because cheaper, AI-enhanced education could unlock massive latent demand for personalized tutoring, remedial support, and enrichment programs that are currently cost-prohibitive, while also enabling teachers to serve more students effectively through AI-powered differentiation and assessment tools.

title: Human resources specialists
rationale: HR specialists face high disruption as core tasks like resume screening, candidate matching, interview scheduling, and policy queries are increasingly automated by AI tools that can process applications, conduct initial screenings, and provide instant policy guidance. However, demand elasticity is low because organizations have relatively fixed HR needs - they don't suddenly need 10x more recruiting or employee relations when these services become cheaper, and regulatory/compliance requirements cap how much efficiency gains translate to expanded services. The combination of high AI disruption of core knowledge work with constrained demand growth points to headcount contraction in this field.

title: Industrial engineering technologists and technicians
rationale: Industrial engineering technologists and technicians face high disruption as their core tasks—data analysis, workflow optimization, statistical studies, and process improvement—are increasingly addressable by AI tools that can analyze production data, suggest layout improvements, and optimize processes. However, their work still requires physical presence on manufacturing floors, interpretation of real-world conditions, and collaboration with teams. Demand elasticity is moderate because while AI-driven efficiency improvements could unlock some latent demand for optimization services across more facilities and processes, manufacturing capacity and regulatory constraints limit how much additional optimization work can be absorbed, leading to a restructured occupation where workers must adapt to AI-augmented analysis tools.

title: Industrial production managers
rationale: Industrial production managers face moderate disruption as AI can assist with production data analysis, scheduling optimization, and quality control monitoring, but the core work requires physical presence on manufacturing floors, interpersonal leadership, and real-time problem-solving in complex industrial environments. Demand elasticity is low because manufacturing capacity and production management needs are largely determined by physical infrastructure and market demand for goods rather than the cost of management services - making production 10x more efficient doesn't create 10x more need for production managers. The disruption score of 6 places this in the resilient category, where AI enhances analytical capabilities but doesn't fundamentally reshape the human-centered leadership and operational oversight requirements.

title: Information clerks
rationale: Information clerks perform highly routine, digital tasks like data entry, record maintenance, form processing, and answering standard inquiries - work that AI and automation can already handle effectively. Most core functions involve structured information processing with minimal physical presence required. Demand for these services is largely inelastic as organizations need a fixed amount of record-keeping, customer inquiries, and administrative processing regardless of cost - making these services 10x cheaper wouldn't generate 10x more demand. The BLS already projects -3% employment decline, reflecting ongoing automation of these routine clerical functions.

title: Insurance sales agents
rationale: Insurance sales involves significant relationship-building, trust establishment, and complex needs assessment that requires human judgment and interpersonal skills, though AI can assist with policy research, client data analysis, and initial screening. While AI tools are becoming useful for lead generation, policy comparison, and administrative tasks, the core work of building client relationships, understanding complex financial situations, and providing personalized advice remains human-centered. Demand elasticity is low because insurance purchases are largely driven by regulatory requirements, life events, and risk management needs rather than price sensitivity - making insurance 10x cheaper wouldn't dramatically increase the number of policies people buy. The moderate disruption score combined with low elasticity places this in the resilient category, as AI enhances but doesn't fundamentally reshape the occupation's core interpersonal and advisory functions.

title: Insurance underwriters
rationale: Insurance underwriting is highly susceptible to AI disruption as it involves analyzing structured data, assessing risk patterns, and making rule-based decisions - all core AI capabilities. The occupation description already mentions heavy reliance on automated underwriting software, and AI can dramatically enhance pattern recognition in risk assessment across medical records, financial histories, and claims data. However, demand elasticity is very low because insurance needs are largely fixed by regulatory requirements, life events, and business necessities - making insurance 10x cheaper wouldn't cause people to buy 10x more policies. The combination of high AI disruption potential with inelastic demand points to significant workforce contraction.

title: Ironworkers
rationale: Ironworking is almost entirely physical work requiring manual dexterity, balance, strength, and real-world presence at construction sites and heights. While AI might assist with peripheral tasks like blueprint reading or project scheduling, the core work of handling, cutting, welding, and positioning heavy steel remains fundamentally hands-on and requires human judgment for safety-critical decisions. Demand elasticity is low because construction projects are driven by economic needs and regulatory requirements rather than cost sensitivity - making steel installation 10x cheaper wouldn't generate 10x more buildings. The physical, safety-critical nature of the work makes this occupation highly resilient to AI disruption.

title: Janitors and building cleaners
rationale: Janitorial work is predominantly physical and hands-on, involving tasks like mopping, vacuuming, trash collection, and basic maintenance that require real-world presence and manual dexterity. While AI might help with scheduling or inventory management, it cannot perform the core cleaning tasks that define this occupation. Demand for cleaning services is relatively inelastic - buildings need a baseline level of cleanliness regardless of cost, and even if cleaning became cheaper, organizations wouldn't dramatically increase their cleaning frequency beyond health and safety requirements. The low disruption score combined with inelastic demand places this firmly in the resilient category.

title: Jewelers and precious stone and metal workers
rationale: The disruption score is moderate because while AI and automation affect some aspects like CAD design and 3D printing for prototyping, the core work remains highly manual - hand crafting, stone setting, polishing, repair work, and gemstone evaluation require physical dexterity, tactile feedback, and human judgment that AI cannot replicate. The elasticity is low because jewelry demand is relatively price-inelastic; people don't buy dramatically more jewelry just because production becomes cheaper, and much of the value comes from craftsmanship, materials, and brand rather than labor costs. With moderate disruption and low elasticity, this occupation falls into the resilient category as AI touches peripheral design tasks but doesn't reshape the fundamentally manual, artisanal core of the work.

title: Judges and hearing officers
rationale: While judges engage in knowledge work like legal research, document review, and decision writing that AI could assist with, the core judicial functions require human judgment, authority, and physical presence in courtrooms that cannot be automated. The role involves complex interpretation of law, managing human interactions, and making consequential decisions that require legitimacy and accountability only humans can provide. Demand for judicial services is highly inelastic - the number of cases requiring judicial resolution is largely fixed by legal requirements and societal needs, not cost considerations, so productivity gains would not meaningfully expand total demand. The moderate disruption combined with inelastic demand places this firmly in the resilient category.

title: Labor relations specialists
rationale: Labor relations specialists face moderate disruption as AI can assist with contract analysis, policy drafting, and research, but the core work requires human judgment, negotiation skills, and relationship management in sensitive workplace disputes. Demand elasticity is low because organizations have relatively fixed needs for labor relations expertise - they don't suddenly need 10x more contract negotiations or grievance procedures just because AI makes analysis faster. The occupation sits at the boundary but falls into 'resilient' because while AI tools will enhance efficiency, the fundamentally human-centered nature of labor negotiations and dispute resolution limits deeper transformation of the role.

title: Landscape architects
rationale: Landscape architects face moderate-to-high disruption as AI increasingly assists with design software, site analysis, environmental modeling, and plan generation, but the core work still requires creative vision, client interaction, and physical site assessment. Demand elasticity is high because cheaper, faster landscape design could unlock significant latent demand from smaller municipalities, businesses, and residential projects that currently can't afford professional landscape architecture services. The combination of meaningful AI assistance with strong demand expansion potential makes this an augmentation scenario where productivity gains drive market growth rather than job displacement.

title: Lawyers
rationale: Legal work is predominantly knowledge-based involving research, document drafting, analysis, and interpretation - all areas where AI tools are already making significant impact through legal research platforms, contract analysis, and document generation. However, demand for legal services faces structural constraints from regulatory requirements, court capacity limits, and the fact that many legal needs (litigation, compliance, transactions) don't scale dramatically even if costs drop. While some latent demand exists for previously unaffordable legal services, the legal system's institutional constraints and the nature of many legal disputes being zero-sum limit demand expansion compared to the productivity gains AI will deliver.

title: Library technicians and assistants
rationale: Library technicians and assistants perform predominantly digital and administrative tasks that AI can increasingly handle - cataloging materials, maintaining databases, answering routine reference questions, and processing loans/returns. Many core functions like searching databases, organizing information, and basic patron assistance are already being automated or enhanced by AI systems. Demand for library services is highly inelastic and actually declining, as evidenced by the BLS projection of -7% employment growth; even if AI made library services dramatically cheaper and faster, people would not consume significantly more library services due to digital alternatives and changing information consumption patterns. The combination of high AI disruption potential and inelastic demand creates conditions where productivity gains translate directly to fewer workers needed.

title: Licensed practical and licensed vocational nurses
rationale: LPNs/LVNs perform predominantly hands-on patient care tasks like monitoring vital signs, changing bandages, bathing patients, and providing comfort care that require physical presence and human interaction. While AI may assist with documentation and some monitoring tasks, the core work remains fundamentally physical and interpersonal. Demand elasticity is high because healthcare needs are expanding with an aging population and chronic conditions, and cheaper care delivery could make services more accessible to underserved populations. However, with disruption at only moderate levels, this occupation falls into the resilient category as AI enhances rather than transforms the core work.

title: Lodging managers
rationale: Lodging managers have moderate disruption as AI can assist with revenue optimization, scheduling, and data analysis, but core duties require physical presence for inspections, face-to-face guest interactions, staff management, and real-time problem resolution. Elasticity is low because demand for lodging management is tied to fixed physical properties and occupancy levels - making operations 10x more efficient doesn't create 10x more hotels or guest stays. The net effect is resilient because while AI tools may enhance operational efficiency and analytics, the fundamental role requires human judgment, physical oversight, and interpersonal skills that remain central to hospitality management.

title: Logisticians
rationale: Logisticians face high disruption as their core work involves data analysis, forecasting, optimization, and coordination—all areas where AI excels through predictive analytics, route optimization algorithms, and automated supply chain management systems. AI can already handle demand forecasting, inventory optimization, and risk monitoring with increasing sophistication. However, demand elasticity is moderate because while cheaper logistics enables more efficient supply chains and could unlock some latent demand for complex multi-modal shipping, the total volume of goods moved is ultimately constrained by underlying economic activity and consumer demand. The net effect is restructure because AI will fundamentally change how logisticians work—shifting them from manual analysis to AI-assisted strategic planning and exception handling—but moderate demand expansion means the occupation survives with transformed skill requirements.

title: Machinists and tool and die makers
rationale: While machinists and tool and die makers use CAD/CAM software and CNC programming, the core work remains fundamentally physical - operating machinery, handling materials, and performing precision manual adjustments that require real-world presence and tactile feedback. AI assists with design and programming aspects but cannot replace the hands-on machining, setup, alignment, and quality verification that defines these roles. Demand elasticity is low because manufacturing output is largely driven by broader economic factors rather than the cost of machining services - companies don't suddenly need 10x more precision parts just because they're cheaper to produce. The moderate disruption combined with structural demand constraints keeps this occupation in the resilient category.

title: Management analysts
rationale: Management analysts perform predominantly knowledge work - analyzing data, developing recommendations, creating presentations, and writing reports - all tasks that AI can significantly enhance or partially automate. AI tools already assist with data analysis, pattern recognition, and report generation, making this a high-disruption occupation. However, demand elasticity is high because cheaper, faster consulting services would unlock significant latent demand from smaller businesses and organizations that currently cannot afford extensive consulting. As AI makes analysis more efficient and accessible, the total market for management consulting services is likely to expand substantially, absorbing productivity gains through increased output rather than workforce reduction.

title: Massage therapists
rationale: Massage therapy is fundamentally a hands-on, physical profession requiring human touch, presence, and real-time assessment of client needs and responses. AI has minimal relevance to the core work of manipulating soft tissues, though it might assist with peripheral tasks like scheduling or basic client intake. The elasticity is high because massage demand is currently constrained by cost and availability - cheaper access through improved efficiency could unlock significant latent demand for wellness and pain management services. However, with disruption at only 2, this occupation falls clearly into the resilient category as AI cannot meaningfully change the core physical work.

title: Material recording clerks
rationale: Material recording clerks perform highly digital, routine tasks like data entry, inventory tracking, barcode scanning, and report compilation that AI and automation can already handle effectively. The occupation is experiencing decline (-6% projected) as RFID tags, automated storage systems, and collaborative robots replace manual tracking functions. Demand for inventory tracking services is relatively inelastic - businesses need accurate inventory records regardless of cost, but they don't need 10x more tracking if it becomes cheaper. Productivity gains from automation translate directly to fewer workers needed rather than expanded demand for tracking services.

title: Materials engineers
rationale: Materials engineers face moderate-to-high disruption as AI increasingly assists with computational modeling, property prediction, and materials discovery through machine learning algorithms, but the core work still requires physical testing, laboratory validation, and real-world application expertise. Demand elasticity is high because cheaper and faster materials development could unlock significant innovation across industries - from aerospace to electronics to renewable energy - where better materials are currently constrained by development costs and time. The combination of meaningful AI assistance with strong demand expansion suggests the field will see productivity gains absorbed through increased output rather than job losses.

title: Mechanical engineering technologists and technicians
rationale: The disruption score is 6 because while AI can assist with design software, CAD work, calculations, and data analysis tasks that comprise a significant portion of the role, the core work still requires substantial hands-on mechanical skills, physical testing, equipment assembly, and real-world problem-solving that AI cannot replicate. The elasticity score is 4 because demand for mechanical engineering support is largely tied to existing manufacturing and engineering projects - while AI-enhanced productivity might enable more thorough testing or design iterations, it won't dramatically expand the total number of mechanical systems that need to be developed or maintained. With moderate disruption and low elasticity, this occupation falls into the resilient category as AI will augment certain tasks but won't fundamentally reshape the core hands-on technical work.

title: Mechanical engineers
rationale: Mechanical engineering involves substantial knowledge work including design, analysis, simulation, and problem-solving that AI tools like CAD automation, simulation software, and design optimization are already enhancing significantly. However, the core work still requires physical system understanding, hands-on prototyping, manufacturing oversight, and real-world problem diagnosis that keeps human expertise central. Demand elasticity is high because cheaper and faster mechanical engineering enables more products to be designed and optimized - from consumer goods to industrial automation - and emerging fields like robotics, renewable energy, and advanced manufacturing create expanding opportunities for mechanical engineering services.

title: Medical and health services managers
rationale: Medical and health services managers face moderate-to-high disruption as AI transforms healthcare administration through automated scheduling, predictive analytics for resource allocation, AI-powered compliance monitoring, and intelligent financial management systems. However, the role requires significant human judgment for strategic decisions, staff leadership, stakeholder relationships, and navigating complex regulatory environments. Demand elasticity is high because healthcare is rapidly expanding due to aging populations, and AI-enabled efficiency improvements will allow organizations to manage larger, more complex operations while reducing administrative costs - unlocking demand for more sophisticated healthcare services and facilities that require skilled management oversight.

title: Medical dosimetrists
rationale: Medical dosimetrists perform highly computational work - calculating radiation doses, analyzing CT/MRI scans, and designing treatment plans - which are core AI capabilities that already show promise in medical imaging and treatment optimization. AI systems are increasingly capable of processing medical images and performing complex dose calculations with high precision. However, demand elasticity is very low because the number of cancer patients requiring radiation therapy is relatively fixed and determined by disease incidence rather than cost or speed of dosimetry services. Making dose calculations 10x faster wouldn't meaningfully increase the total number of radiation treatments needed, as this is constrained by patient population and medical necessity rather than dosimetrist availability.

title: Medical records specialists
rationale: Medical records specialists perform almost entirely digital tasks - reviewing records, coding diagnoses, data entry, and maintaining electronic health records - all of which are prime targets for AI automation. AI can already handle much of the pattern recognition, coding classification, and data processing that forms the core of this work. Demand elasticity is very low because healthcare organizations need a fixed amount of medical coding and record maintenance regardless of cost - patients don't generate more medical records just because processing becomes cheaper, and regulatory requirements for record-keeping remain constant. The BLS already acknowledges that AI-powered solutions are affecting demand for these workers.

title: Medical scientists
rationale: Medical scientists face high disruption as AI increasingly assists with data analysis, literature review, hypothesis generation, experimental design, and pattern recognition in biological data - core cognitive tasks that comprise much of their work. However, demand elasticity is high because cheaper, faster research capabilities could dramatically expand medical research across underserved diseases, personalized medicine, and global health challenges where current research is limited by cost and complexity. The combination of high disruption with high elasticity suggests medical scientists will be augmented rather than replaced, with each scientist able to conduct far more research projects and investigations than currently possible.

title: Metal and plastic machine workers
rationale: While AI and automation are advancing in manufacturing (CNC programming, predictive maintenance, quality control), the core work remains predominantly physical - operating heavy machinery, handling materials, and performing hands-on setup and maintenance tasks that require real-world presence and manual dexterity. The disruption is moderate as AI assists with programming and monitoring but doesn't replace the fundamental physical operations. Demand elasticity is low because manufacturing output is driven by broader economic demand for goods rather than the cost of machine operation; making machining 10x cheaper wouldn't dramatically increase total demand for manufactured parts. The net effect is resilient as AI enhances but doesn't fundamentally reshape the core physical work.

title: Military careers
rationale: Military careers involve substantial physical presence, combat readiness, leadership in unpredictable environments, and human judgment in life-or-death situations that AI cannot replicate. While AI may assist with logistics, intelligence analysis, and administrative tasks, the core military functions of physical operations, command decisions under stress, and maintaining unit cohesion remain fundamentally human. Demand for military personnel is determined by geopolitical threats and national security needs rather than economic efficiency, making it highly inelastic to productivity improvements. The occupation remains resilient as AI touches only peripheral support functions while core military duties require irreplaceable human capabilities.

title: Models
rationale: AI disruption is high because core modeling tasks—posing for photos, displaying products, and creating visual content—are increasingly being replaced by AI-generated imagery, virtual models, and deepfake technology that can create realistic human likenesses without human models. Demand elasticity is low because the need for product promotion and advertising is relatively fixed; companies don't dramatically increase their marketing spend just because AI models are cheaper, and regulatory/brand authenticity concerns limit adoption. The combination of high disruption and low elasticity points to significant headcount contraction as AI-generated models become more sophisticated and widely accepted.

title: Musicians and singers
rationale: AI significantly disrupts music creation through composition tools, vocal synthesis, and production software, but live performance and human emotional connection remain central to the occupation. Demand elasticity is high because AI-powered tools dramatically lower barriers to music creation and distribution, enabling more independent artists to produce and share music while expanding opportunities for personalized content, background music, and niche genres. The combination of meaningful AI disruption in production workflows with strong demand expansion from reduced costs and democratized creation tools leads to an augment effect where musicians can produce more diverse content for expanded markets.

title: Network and computer systems administrators
rationale: Network and systems administration involves predominantly digital tasks like configuring software, managing servers, monitoring systems, and troubleshooting - all areas where AI automation tools are rapidly advancing and already being deployed. The BLS projects a 4% decline partly due to automation of routine tasks and outsourcing to managed services. Demand elasticity is low because organizations have relatively fixed infrastructure needs that don't expand significantly even if administration becomes cheaper - a company doesn't need 10x more network management just because it's more affordable. The combination of high AI disruption potential with constrained demand growth points to workforce contraction.

title: Nuclear engineers
rationale: Nuclear engineering involves significant knowledge work including design, analysis, and regulatory compliance that AI can assist with, but the core work requires deep domain expertise, safety-critical decision making, and physical oversight of nuclear facilities that AI cannot replace. The field has strict regulatory requirements and involves life-safety considerations that limit AI's direct application to core tasks. Demand elasticity is low because nuclear power deployment is constrained by regulatory approval processes, public acceptance, capital requirements, and policy decisions rather than engineering costs - making nuclear services 10x cheaper wouldn't dramatically expand nuclear facility construction or operations. The disruption score of 6 places this in the resilient category despite some AI assistance potential.

title: Nuclear medicine technologists
rationale: Nuclear medicine technologists have moderate disruption because while AI can assist with image analysis and record-keeping, the core work requires hands-on preparation and administration of radioactive drugs, direct patient interaction, and strict safety protocols that demand human oversight. The work is fundamentally physical and safety-critical, limiting AI's ability to replace core functions. Demand elasticity is low because nuclear medicine procedures are medically necessary rather than elective - patients don't get 10x more nuclear scans just because AI makes analysis faster or cheaper, and the procedures are already ordered based on clinical need rather than cost constraints. The combination of moderate disruption and low elasticity places this occupation in the resilient category, where AI may improve efficiency in peripheral tasks but won't fundamentally reshape the profession.

title: Nuclear technicians
rationale: Nuclear technicians perform primarily hands-on monitoring, maintenance, and safety work in highly regulated physical environments that require human presence for safety and regulatory compliance. While AI could assist with data analysis and pattern recognition in radiation monitoring, the core work involves physical equipment operation, sample collection, and real-time safety responses that cannot be automated. Demand for nuclear technician services is highly inelastic - nuclear facilities have fixed regulatory requirements for human oversight and safety monitoring regardless of cost, and the declining nuclear industry limits any potential demand expansion from productivity gains.

title: Nursing assistants and orderlies
rationale: Nursing assistants and orderlies perform predominantly physical, hands-on care tasks like bathing patients, helping with mobility, cleaning, and providing emotional support through direct human interaction. While AI might assist with some documentation or monitoring tasks, the core work requires physical presence, manual dexterity, and human empathy that AI cannot replicate. Demand elasticity is high because healthcare needs are expanding with an aging population, and if costs were reduced, more people could access quality care in various settings. However, since disruption is low (mostly physical work with minimal digital components), this occupation falls into the resilient category despite high demand elasticity.

title: Occupational health and safety specialists and technicians
rationale: While AI can assist with data analysis, report writing, and pattern recognition in safety incidents, the core work requires physical workplace inspections, hands-on equipment testing, human judgment for complex safety assessments, and in-person training delivery. The occupation involves substantial fieldwork, physical presence at worksites, and regulatory compliance that demands human oversight. Demand shows moderate elasticity as cheaper safety analysis could expand services to smaller businesses and enable more frequent inspections, but growth is constrained by regulatory requirements and the inherently site-specific nature of safety work. The disruption score of 5 places this in the resilient category despite moderate demand elasticity.

title: Occupational therapists
rationale: Occupational therapy is fundamentally about hands-on, personalized human interaction - evaluating clients through observation, demonstrating physical exercises, teaching daily living skills, and providing emotional support during rehabilitation. While AI might assist with documentation, treatment planning, or equipment recommendations, the core work requires physical presence, empathy, and real-time adaptation to individual client needs. The elasticity is high because cheaper/faster therapy services could dramatically expand access - many people currently can't afford adequate rehabilitation, and an aging population creates massive latent demand for independence-supporting services. However, since disruption remains moderate, this falls into the resilient category where AI enhances rather than transforms the occupation.

title: Occupational therapy assistants and aides
rationale: This occupation involves predominantly hands-on, physical therapy work requiring direct human interaction, physical manipulation of clients, and real-time assessment of patient responses - areas where AI has minimal impact. While AI might assist with some administrative tasks like scheduling and documentation, the core therapeutic activities of guiding exercises, teaching adaptive techniques, and providing physical assistance remain fundamentally human-centered. Demand elasticity is high due to an aging population and cost pressures driving healthcare providers to use more assistants, but the low disruption score means this falls into the resilient category where AI touches only the periphery of the work.

title: Oil and gas workers
rationale: Oil and gas work is predominantly physical and hands-on, involving equipment operation, maintenance, and repair in harsh outdoor environments that require human presence and adaptability. While AI and automation may assist with monitoring and some remote operations, the core tasks of assembling drilling equipment, performing maintenance, and coordinating physical operations remain largely unchanged. Demand for oil and gas extraction is relatively inelastic - it's driven by global energy needs rather than cost efficiency, and productivity gains from AI don't significantly expand total extraction demand. The low disruption score combined with inelastic demand places this occupation firmly in the resilient category.

title: Painters, construction and maintenance
rationale: Painting is fundamentally a physical, hands-on craft requiring manual dexterity, surface preparation, tool handling, and real-world presence at job sites. While AI might assist with peripheral tasks like color matching or project estimation, the core work of applying coatings, working at heights, and adapting to varied surface conditions remains entirely manual. rationale: Welding is fundamentally a physical, hands-on craft requiring manual dexterity, spatial orientation, and real-world presence to manipulate materials and equipment in often unpredictable environments. While AI might assist with peripheral tasks like blueprint interpretation or process optimization, the core work of applying heat, monitoring molten metal flow, and ensuring proper joint formation remains inherently manual and requires human judgment in real-time. Demand elasticity is low-to-moderate because welding needs are largely tied to fixed infrastructure projects and manufacturing requirements that don't dramatically expand even if costs decrease. The occupation remains resilient as AI cannot meaningfully replace the core physical and sensory skills required for quality welding work.

title: Wholesale and manufacturing sales representatives
rationale: AI significantly disrupts the knowledge-work components of sales - lead generation, customer research, proposal writing, and administrative tasks are increasingly automated, while CRM systems with AI insights change how representatives prioritize and approach prospects. However, the core relationship-building, complex negotiation, and trust-establishment aspects still require human presence, especially for high-value B2B sales. Demand elasticity is limited because businesses have relatively fixed procurement needs and regulatory/structural constraints in purchasing processes, though some expansion may occur as AI makes sales processes more efficient and accessible to smaller vendors. The combination of high disruption to sales processes but constrained demand growth suggests the occupation will survive but require substantial skill adaptation toward relationship management and strategic consultation.

title: Writers and authors
rationale: Writers and authors face very high disruption as their core work—creating written content, developing narratives, and crafting language—is directly in AI's capability path, with AI tools already materially changing how writing is done across all formats from blogs to screenplays. Demand elasticity is high because cheaper, faster content creation unlocks significant latent demand across digital media, marketing, entertainment, and personalized content where cost barriers previously limited production volume. While AI can generate much of the raw content, the combination of high disruption with high elasticity suggests the occupation will be augmented rather than replaced, as expanded content demand absorbs productivity gains.

title: Zoologists and wildlife biologists
rationale: Disruption is moderate because while AI can assist with data analysis, modeling, and report writing, the core work requires extensive fieldwork, direct animal observation, specimen collection, and hands-on research in natural environments that AI cannot replace. Elasticity is low because demand for wildlife research is primarily driven by conservation needs and government funding rather than cost considerations - making research 10x cheaper wouldn't dramatically expand the number of species studied or conservation projects undertaken due to regulatory and budgetary constraints. The net effect is resilient since AI touches peripheral tasks but doesn't reshape the fundamentally field-based, observational nature of the work.
